title: Short title, commencement and application
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
description: Provides the name of the Act, commencement provisions, territorial application, and extra-territorial jurisdiction.

subsections.text: This Act may be called the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
The Act shall come into force on a date notified by the Central Government.
Every person is liable to punishment under this Sanhita for acts or omissions committed within India.
Persons liable under Indian law for offences committed outside India may be dealt with under this Sanhita.
Applies to Indian citizens abroad, persons on Indian registered ships or aircraft, and offences targeting computer resources located in India.
Does not affect special laws relating to mutiny, desertion, or other special/local laws.

illustrations: An Indian citizen committing murder outside India may be tried and convicted in India.
ai_summary: Defines where and to whom the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita applies, including certain offences committed outside India.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
description: Defines important terms used throughout the Sanhita.

important_definitions.meaning: Includes a series of acts as well as a single act.
Any person below eighteen years of age.
Includes electronic and digital records used as evidence.
Any illegal harm to body, mind, reputation or property.
An act made punishable under the Sanhita.
Includes companies, associations and bodies of persons.
Includes judges, government officers and other specified officials.
Gain by unlawful means of property.
Loss by unlawful means of property.

ai_summary: Provides definitions that apply throughout the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.

title: General explanations
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
description: Explains how provisions of the Sanhita should be interpreted.

subsections.text: All offences and punishments are subject to the General Exceptions.
Expressions explained in one part carry the same meaning throughout the Sanhita.
Property possessed by spouse, clerk or servant may be treated as possession of the person concerned.
References to acts include illegal omissions.
Joint participation with criminal knowledge creates liability.
An offence may be committed partly by an act and partly by an omission.
Intentional cooperation in an offence creates criminal responsibility.
Different participants in the same act may be guilty of different offences.

ai_summary: Provides interpretation rules used throughout the Sanhita.

title: Punishments
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
description: Lists punishments available under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.
ai_summary: Enumerates all forms of punishment recognised under the Sanhita.

title: Commutation of sentence
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
description: Empowers the appropriate Government to commute punishments in accordance with law.
ai_summary: Allows legally authorised commutation of punishments by the appropriate Government.

title: Fractions of terms of punishment
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
content: In calculating fractions of terms of punishment, imprisonment for life shall be reckoned as equivalent to imprisonment for twenty years unless otherwise provided.
ai_summary: For calculating fractions of punishment, life imprisonment is treated as twenty years unless otherwise stated.

title: Sentence may be wholly or partly rigorous or simple
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
content: Where an offender is punishable with imprisonment of either description, the Court may direct that the imprisonment shall be wholly rigorous, wholly simple, or partly rigorous and partly simple.
ai_summary: The Court can decide whether imprisonment will be rigorous, simple, or a combination of both.

title: Amount of fine, liability in default of payment of fine, etc.
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
content: Provides rules regarding fines, imprisonment in default of payment of fine or community service, limits on such imprisonment, recovery of unpaid fines, and termination of imprisonment when the fine is paid.
important_points: Fine may be unlimited unless specifically restricted.
Court may order imprisonment if fine is not paid.
Default imprisonment has statutory limits.
Fine can be recovered even after sentence within prescribed period.
ai_summary: Explains how fines are imposed, recovered, and the consequences of non-payment.

title: Limit of punishment of offence made up of several offences
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
content: Where an offence consists of several acts or falls under multiple definitions, the offender shall not be punished more than once for the same act unless expressly provided by law.
ai_summary: Prevents excessive punishment when the same conduct constitutes multiple offences.

title: Punishment when guilty of one of several offences and doubt exists
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
content: Where it is uncertain which of several offences has been committed, the offender shall receive the punishment prescribed for the offence carrying the lowest punishment if punishments differ.
ai_summary: When there is doubt about which offence was committed, the least severe applicable punishment is imposed.

title: Solitary confinement
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
content: When a person is sentenced to rigorous imprisonment, the Court may order solitary confinement for a limited period. The maximum period depends on the total term of imprisonment and cannot exceed three months in total.
ai_summary: Allows courts to impose limited solitary confinement along with rigorous imprisonment.

title: Limit of solitary confinement
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
content: Solitary confinement cannot exceed fourteen days at one time. Required intervals must exist between periods of solitary confinement. Additional limits apply where imprisonment exceeds three months.
ai_summary: Places restrictions on how long and how frequently solitary confinement may be used.

title: Enhanced punishment for certain offences after previous conviction
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
content: A person previously convicted of certain serious offences and later convicted again for similar offences may receive enhanced punishment, including imprisonment for life or imprisonment extending up to ten years.
ai_summary: Provides stricter punishment for repeat offenders convicted of specified offences.

title: Act done by a person bound, or by mistake of fact believing himself bound, by law
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing is an offence if done by a person who is bound by law to do it, or who in good faith believes, because of a mistake of fact and not a mistake of law, that he is bound by law to do it.
illustration: A soldier firing on a mob under lawful orders commits no offence.
ai_summary: Protects persons who act under legal duty or a genuine mistake of fact regarding legal duty.

title: Act of Judge when acting judicially
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing is an offence when done by a Judge acting judicially in the exercise of powers given by law or believed in good faith to be given by law.
ai_summary: Provides legal protection to judges performing judicial functions in good faith.

title: Act done pursuant to judgment or order of Court
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing is an offence which is done in pursuance of, or warranted by, the judgment or order of a Court, provided the person acts in good faith believing that the Court had jurisdiction.
ai_summary: A person acting under a Court's order in good faith is protected from criminal liability.

title: Act done by a person justified, or by mistake of fact believing himself justified, by law
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing is an offence if done by a person who is justified by law, or who in good faith believes due to a mistake of fact that he is justified by law.
illustration: A person apprehending someone believed to have committed murder acts under legal justification.
ai_summary: Protects persons who lawfully act or honestly believe they are legally justified.

title: Accident in doing a lawful act
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune without criminal intention or knowledge while performing a lawful act in a lawful manner with proper care and caution.
illustration: A worker using a hatchet whose head accidentally flies off and causes death may not be criminally liable if proper care was taken.
ai_summary: Accidental harm caused during a lawful act with proper care is not an offence.

title: Act likely to cause harm, but done without criminal intent and to prevent other harm
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: An act done in good faith to prevent greater harm is not an offence merely because it is likely to cause some harm, provided there is no criminal intention.
illustrations: Changing a ship's course to save many lives despite risking fewer lives.
Pulling down houses to stop a large fire from spreading.
ai_summary: Actions taken in good faith to avoid a greater danger may be legally excused.

title: Act of a child under seven years of age
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under seven years of age.
ai_summary: Children below seven years of age cannot be held criminally liable under BNS.

title: Act of a child above seven and under twelve years of age of immature understanding
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above seven years of age and under twelve years of age who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge the nature and consequences of his conduct.
ai_summary: A child between 7 and 12 years is not criminally liable if incapable of understanding the nature and consequences of the act.

title: Act of a person of unsound mind
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing is an offence if done by a person who, due to unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act or that the act is wrong or contrary to law.
ai_summary: A person suffering from unsoundness of mind may not be criminally liable if incapable of understanding the act.

title: Act of a person incapable of judgment by reason of intoxication caused against his will
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing is an offence if done by a person incapable of understanding the nature of the act due to intoxication administered without his knowledge or against his will.
ai_summary: Protection applies when intoxication was involuntary and prevented proper judgment.

title: Offence requiring a particular intent or knowledge committed by one who is intoxicated
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: A person committing an act while intoxicated is treated as having the same knowledge as if not intoxicated, unless the intoxication was administered without knowledge or against the person's will.
ai_summary: Voluntary intoxication is generally not a defence to crimes requiring intent or knowledge.

title: Act not intended and not known to be likely to cause death or grievous hurt, done by consent
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: An act causing harm is not an offence where the person suffering harm is above eighteen years of age, consents to the risk, and the act is neither intended nor known to be likely to cause death or grievous hurt.
illustrations: Two adults fencing for amusement consent to ordinary risks arising from the activity.
ai_summary: An adult who voluntarily accepts ordinary risks cannot generally complain of harm resulting from those risks.

title: Act not intended to cause death, done by consent in good faith for person's benefit
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Nothing which is not intended to cause death is an offence by reason of any harm caused to a person for whose benefit it is done in good faith, provided that person has given express or implied consent.
illustrations: A surgeon performs a risky operation in good faith with the patient's consent for the patient's benefit.
ai_summary: Good-faith acts done for a person's benefit with consent are generally not offences.

title: Act done in good faith for benefit of child or person of unsound mind by consent of guardian
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Acts done in good faith for the benefit of a child under twelve years of age or a person of unsound mind, with consent of the guardian or lawful custodian, are generally not offences subject to specified limitations.
exceptions: Intentional causing of death
Attempt to cause death
Causing grievous hurt except in limited circumstances
illustrations: A guardian authorises a surgeon to perform an operation necessary for a child's health.
ai_summary: Protects good-faith actions taken for children or persons of unsound mind with guardian consent.

title: Consent known to be given under fear or misconception
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Consent is not valid if obtained through fear of injury, misconception of fact, unsoundness of mind, intoxication, or where the person is under twelve years of age unless the context provides otherwise.
ai_summary: Explains when consent is legally invalid.

title: Exclusion of acts which are offences independently of harm caused
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: The exceptions relating to consent do not apply to acts that are offences independently of the harm caused.
illustrations: Consent does not legalise acts that are independently criminal under law.
ai_summary: Consent cannot be used as a defence for offences that are independently punishable.

title: Act done in good faith for benefit of a person without consent
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Acts done in good faith for a person's benefit may not be offences when obtaining consent is impossible and immediate action is necessary, subject to statutory limitations.
illustrations: A surgeon performs an emergency operation on an unconscious person.
A person takes emergency action to save another from immediate danger.
exceptions: Intentional causing of death
Acts known to be likely to cause death except in permitted situations
ai_summary: Allows emergency actions taken in good faith when consent cannot be obtained.

title: Communication made in good faith
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: No communication made in good faith for the benefit of a person is an offence merely because it may cause harm to that person.
illustrations: A doctor honestly informs a patient about a serious medical condition.
ai_summary: Truthful communication made in good faith for a person's benefit is protected.

title: Act to which a person is compelled by threats
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: Except for murder and certain offences against the State punishable with death, an act done under threat of instant death may not be an offence if the person had no reasonable alternative.
exceptions: Murder
Offences against the State punishable with death
ai_summary: A person forced to act under immediate threat of death may receive legal protection.

title: Act causing slight harm
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
content: An act is not an offence if the harm caused is so slight that a reasonable person would not complain of it.
ai_summary: Very minor or trivial harm is generally not treated as a criminal offence.

title: Things done in private defence
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: Nothing is an offence if it is done in the lawful exercise of the right of private defence.
ai_summary: Lawful self-defence is not a criminal offence.

title: Right of private defence of body and property
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: Every person has the right to defend their own body, another person's body, and movable or immovable property against specified offences such as theft, robbery, mischief, and criminal trespass.
ai_summary: Explains the legal right to protect people and property from unlawful acts.

title: Right of private defence against act of a person of unsound mind, etc.
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: When an act would otherwise be an offence but is not an offence because of the youth, immaturity of understanding, unsoundness of mind, intoxication, or misconception of the person doing it, every person has the same right of private defence against that act as if the act were an offence.
illustrations: A may exercise private defence against Z, a person of unsound mind, who attempts to kill him.
A may exercise private defence against Z who attacks him under a mistaken belief.
conditions: The act must create a threat that would amount to an offence if committed by a legally responsible person.
ai_summary: Self-defence is available even when the aggressor is legally incapable of committing an offence.

title: Acts against which there is no right of private defence
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: There is no right of private defence against an act of a public servant acting in good faith under colour of office, or against an act done under the direction of such public servant, unless there is reasonable apprehension of death or grievous hurt. There is also no right where there is sufficient time to seek protection from public authorities.
limitations: No right against certain acts of public servants acting in good faith.
No right when public authority protection is reasonably available.
The right never extends to inflicting more harm than necessary.
ai_summary: Defines situations where private defence cannot be claimed and limits the amount of force that may be used.

title: When right of private defence of body extends to causing death
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: The right of private defence of the body extends to voluntarily causing death of the assailant when the assault creates a reasonable apprehension of death, grievous hurt, rape, gratification of unnatural lust, kidnapping, abduction, wrongful confinement preventing recourse to authorities, or acid attack.
grounds: Assault with intention to commit rape
Assault with intention to gratify unnatural lust
Assault with intention to kidnap or abduct
Wrongful confinement preventing access to public authorities
Acid attack or attempted acid attack
ai_summary: Lists exceptional situations where causing the death of an attacker may be legally justified.

title: When such right extends to causing any harm other than death
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: If the offence is not one of the offences specified in Section 38, the right of private defence does not extend to causing death but extends to causing any harm other than death, subject to the restrictions in Section 37.
ai_summary: For ordinary threats, only proportionate non-fatal force may be used.

title: Commencement and continuance of right of private defence of body
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: The right of private defence of the body commences as soon as a reasonable apprehension of danger arises from an attempt or threat to commit an offence, even before the offence is committed, and continues as long as such apprehension continues.
conditions: A threat or attempt is sufficient.
The right continues only while the danger reasonably exists.
ai_summary: Explains when the right of self-defence begins and when it ends.

title: When right of private defence of property extends to causing death
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: The right of private defence of property extends to voluntarily causing death or any other harm to the wrongdoer if the offence committed or attempted is robbery, house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, mischief by fire or explosive substance to a dwelling or place for custody of property, or theft, mischief or house-trespass under circumstances causing reasonable apprehension of death or grievous hurt.
grounds: House-breaking after sunset and before sunrise
Mischief by fire or explosive substance
Theft causing apprehension of death or grievous hurt
House-trespass causing apprehension of death or grievous hurt
ai_summary: In certain serious offences against property, the law permits even causing the death of the wrongdoer.

title: When such right extends to causing any harm other than death
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: If the offence is theft, mischief or criminal trespass not falling under Section 41, the right of private defence extends only to causing harm other than death, subject to Section 37.
ai_summary: For ordinary property offences, only necessary non-fatal force may be used.

title: Commencement and continuance of right of private defence of property
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: The right of private defence of property begins when reasonable apprehension of danger to property arises and continues according to the nature of the offence.

continuance_rules.theft: Continues until the offender retreats with the property, public authorities intervene, or the property is recovered.
continuance_rules.robbery: Continues while fear of death, hurt or wrongful restraint exists.
continuance_rules.criminal_trespass: Continues as long as trespass continues.
continuance_rules.mischief: Continues as long as mischief continues.

ai_summary: Explains when the right to defend property begins and ends.

title: Right of private defence against deadly assault when there is risk of harm to innocent person
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
content: If a person cannot effectively exercise the right of private defence against an assault creating reasonable apprehension of death without risking harm to an innocent person, the right extends to running that risk.
illustration: A person attacked by a murderous mob may fire in self-defence even if there is a risk of accidentally harming an innocent person mixed with the mob.
ai_summary: The law may excuse unavoidable risk to innocent persons during lawful self-defence against a deadly attack.

title: Abetment of a thing
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: A person abets the doing of a thing if he instigates another person to do it, engages in a conspiracy for doing it and an act or illegal omission occurs in pursuance of that conspiracy, or intentionally aids the doing of that thing.
important_explanations: Wilful misrepresentation or concealment of material facts may amount to instigation.
Facilitating an act before or during its commission may amount to aiding.

punishment.imprisonment: As provided in subsequent abetment sections

ai_summary: Defines the three legal methods by which a person can abet an offence.

title: Abettor
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: A person abets an offence if he abets the commission of an offence or an act which would be an offence if committed by a person capable by law of committing that offence with the same intention or knowledge.
important_points: Actual commission of the offence is not necessary.
A child or person of unsound mind may be used by an abettor.
Abetment itself is an offence.
ai_summary: Defines who is considered an abettor under BNS.

title: Abetment in India of offences outside India
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: A person in India who abets the commission of an act outside India which would be an offence if committed in India commits abetment under this Sanhita.
illustration: A in India instigates B in another country to commit murder.
ai_summary: Abetment from India is punishable even if the offence occurs abroad.

title: Abetment outside India for offence in India
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: A person outside India who abets the commission of an act in India which would constitute an offence if committed in India commits abetment under this Sanhita.
illustration: A in a foreign country instigates B to commit murder in India.
ai_summary: Abetment committed abroad for an offence in India is punishable.

title: Punishment of abetment if act abetted is committed in consequence and where no express provision is made
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: Where the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment and no specific punishment is provided, the abettor shall be punished with the punishment prescribed for that offence.
ai_summary: If the offence is committed due to abetment, the abettor generally receives the same punishment as the offender.

title: Punishment of abetment if person abetted does act with different intention from that of abettor
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: If the person abetted performs the act with a different intention or knowledge from that of the abettor, the abettor is punished according to the offence that would have been committed had the act been done with the abettor's intention or knowledge.

punishment.imprisonment: Depends on offence attributable to abettor

ai_summary: The abettor's punishment is determined by the intention or knowledge he possessed.

title: Liability of abettor when one act abetted and different act done
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: When one act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act actually done if it was a probable consequence of the abetment and was committed under the influence of the instigation, aid or conspiracy.
ai_summary: An abettor may be punished for a different offence if it was a probable consequence of the abetment.

title: Abettor when liable to cumulative punishment for act abetted and for act done
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: If the act done in consequence of abetment is in addition to the act abetted and constitutes a separate offence, the abettor may be punished for both offences.
ai_summary: An abettor can be punished separately for multiple offences arising from the same abetment.

title: Liability of abettor for effect caused different from effect intended
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: Where an act is abetted with the intention of causing a particular effect, but a different effect results, the abettor is liable for the effect actually caused if it was a probable consequence of the act.
ai_summary: Liability may extend to unintended consequences that naturally result from the abetted act.

title: Abettor present when offence is committed
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: Whenever an abettor is present when the offence abetted is committed, the abettor is deemed to have committed that offence.
ai_summary: An abettor present at the scene is treated as if they committed the offence themselves.

title: Abetment of offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life if offence not committed
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: Whoever abets the commission of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, and the offence is not committed, is punishable under this section.
ai_summary: Abetting a very serious offence is punishable even if the offence is never committed.

title: Abetment of offence punishable with imprisonment
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: Whoever abets an offence punishable with imprisonment, if the offence is not committed and no specific punishment is provided, shall be punished accordingly.

imprisonment.maximum: One-fourth of the longest term prescribed for the offence

punishment.fine: Same fine as provided for the offence

public_servant_case.maximum_imprisonment: One-half of the longest term prescribed for the offence
public_servant_case.fine: Same fine as provided for the offence

illustrations: Instigating false evidence even if not given.
Police officer abetting robbery though robbery is not committed.
ai_summary: Abetment of an imprisonable offence is punishable even when the offence is not committed.

title: Abetting commission of offence by public or by more than ten persons
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: Whoever abets the commission of an offence by the public generally or by any class or group exceeding ten persons commits an offence.
illustration: Publicly instigating a group of more than ten persons to attack another group.
ai_summary: Inciting large groups of people to commit offences is punishable with up to seven years imprisonment and fine.

title: Concealing design to commit offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: Whoever intentionally conceals, including through encryption or information hiding tools, a plan to commit an offence punishable with death or life imprisonment.
ai_summary: Concealing plans for very serious crimes is punishable even if the crime is never committed.

title: Public servant concealing design to commit offence which it is his duty to prevent
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: A public servant who intentionally conceals a planned offence that it is his duty to prevent commits an offence.

if_offence_committed.imprisonment: Up to one-half of the longest term prescribed for that offence

if_offence_not_committed.imprisonment: Up to one-fourth of the longest term prescribed for that offence

ai_summary: Public servants face enhanced liability for hiding offences they are legally bound to prevent.

title: Concealing design to commit offence punishable with imprisonment
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: Whoever conceals a plan to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment, or knowingly makes false representations regarding such a plan, commits an offence.

if_offence_committed.imprisonment: Up to one-fourth of the longest term prescribed for the offence

if_offence_not_committed.imprisonment: Up to one-eighth of the longest term prescribed for the offence

ai_summary: Concealing plans for ordinary imprisonable offences is punishable, with greater punishment if the offence is actually committed.

title: Criminal Conspiracy
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: When two or more persons agree to do an illegal act, or a legal act by illegal means, such agreement constitutes criminal conspiracy. Except for agreements to commit an offence, an overt act in pursuance of the agreement is required.
important_points: Agreement between two or more persons
Illegal act or legal act by illegal means
Overt act required unless conspiracy is to commit an offence

if_conspiracy_for_offence_punishable_wit.punishment: Same as abetment of that offence

ai_summary: An agreement to commit an illegal act is criminal conspiracy.

title: Punishment for attempting to commit offences punishable with imprisonment for life or other imprisonment
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
content: Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment and performs any act towards its commission shall be punished where no specific punishment for attempt exists.
illustrations: Attempting theft by opening a jewel box even when no jewels are found.
Attempting pickpocketing even though the pocket is empty.

imprisonment.maximum: One-half of life imprisonment or one-half of the longest term prescribed for the offence

punishment.fine: Same fine as prescribed for the offence

ai_summary: A person can be punished for attempting a crime even if the crime is not completed.

title: Rape
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: Defines rape and specifies the circumstances under which sexual acts constitute rape, including acts against a woman's will, without consent, under coercion, fraud, intoxication, incapacity, or where the woman is under eighteen years of age.
important_points: Consent must be voluntary and unequivocal.
A woman under 18 years cannot legally consent.
Lack of physical resistance does not imply consent.
Medical procedures are excluded.
exceptions: Medical procedure or intervention.
Sexual intercourse by a man with his wife, provided the wife is not under eighteen years of age.

punishment.reason: Definition section. Punishment provided in Section 64.

ai_summary: Defines rape, consent, and the circumstances under which sexual acts become criminal.

title: Punishment for Rape
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: Provides punishment for rape and aggravated forms of rape committed by police officers, public servants, armed forces personnel, jail staff, hospital staff, guardians, teachers, persons in authority, during communal violence, on pregnant women, disabled women, or repeated rape.

rigorous_imprisonment.maximum: Life Imprisonment for remainder of natural life

ai_summary: Punishes rape with a minimum of ten years imprisonment and enhanced punishment for aggravated cases.

title: Punishment for Rape on Woman Under Sixteen Years of Age
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: Provides enhanced punishment where the victim is below sixteen years of age or below twelve years of age.

rigorous_imprisonment.maximum: Life Imprisonment for remainder of natural life
Life Imprisonment for remainder of natural life

ai_summary: Provides extremely severe punishment, including possible death penalty, for rape of very young children.

title: Punishment for causing death or resulting in persistent vegetative state of victim
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: Whoever commits rape under Section 64 and during the commission causes injury resulting in the death of the woman or causes her to remain in a persistent vegetative state shall be punished.

rigorous_imprisonment.maximum: Life Imprisonment for remainder of natural life

ai_summary: If rape results in the victim's death or leaves her in a persistent vegetative state, the offender faces a minimum of twenty years imprisonment, life imprisonment, or death.

title: Sexual intercourse by husband upon his wife during separation
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: A husband who has sexual intercourse with his wife without her consent while living separately, whether under a decree of separation or otherwise, commits an offence.
important_points: Applies only when spouses are living separately.
Consent of the wife is mandatory.
Sexual intercourse includes acts under clauses (a) to (d) of Section 63.
ai_summary: Non-consensual sexual intercourse by a husband with a wife living separately is punishable.

title: Sexual intercourse by a person in authority
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: A person in authority, fiduciary relationship, public servant, jail staff, institution staff, hospital management or hospital staff who abuses such position to induce or seduce a woman into sexual intercourse, where the act does not amount to rape, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Abusing a position of authority to obtain sexual intercourse is punishable even when the act does not amount to rape.

title: Sexual intercourse by employing deceitful means, etc.
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: Whoever has sexual intercourse with a woman by deceitful means or by making a promise to marry without any intention of fulfilling it, where the act does not amount to rape, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Sexual intercourse obtained through deception or a false promise of marriage is punishable with up to ten years imprisonment.

title: Gang Rape
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: Where a woman is raped by one or more persons acting as a group or with a common intention, every member of the group is deemed to have committed rape.

rigorous_imprisonment.maximum: Life Imprisonment for remainder of natural life

ai_summary: Gang rape attracts extremely severe punishment, including life imprisonment and, in certain cases involving minors, the death penalty.

title: Punishment for Repeat Offenders
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: Whoever has previously been convicted under Section 64, 65, 66, or 70 and is subsequently convicted again under any of those sections shall be punished as a repeat offender.

punishment.life_imprisonment: Imprisonment for remainder of natural life

ai_summary: Repeat convictions for serious rape offences can result in life imprisonment for the remainder of natural life or death.

title: Disclosure of Identity of Victim of Certain Offences
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: Printing or publishing the name or any information that reveals the identity of a victim of offences under Sections 64 to 71 is prohibited, except in specified circumstances authorised by law.
exceptions: Publication authorised by investigating police officer for investigation purposes.
Publication authorised in writing by the victim.
Where victim is dead, a child, or of unsound mind, authorisation by next of kin.
Authorisation by next of kin can only be given to recognised welfare organisations.
ai_summary: Protects the identity of victims of sexual offences and penalises unauthorised disclosure.

title: Publication of Proceedings Relating to Certain Offences
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman and Child
content: Publishing any matter relating to court proceedings concerning offences covered under Section 72 without prior permission of the court is prohibited.
exceptions: Publication of judgments of High Courts.
Publication of judgments of the Supreme Court.
ai_summary: Restricts publication of proceedings related to certain sexual offence cases without court approval.

title: Assault or Criminal Force to Woman with Intent to Outrage Her Modesty
chapter: Chapter V - Of Criminal Force and Assault Against Woman
content: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force against a woman intending to outrage, or knowing it is likely to outrage, her modesty commits an offence.
ai_summary: Assaulting or using criminal force against a woman with intent to outrage her modesty is punishable.

title: Sexual Harassment
chapter: Chapter V - Of Criminal Force and Assault Against Woman
content: Sexual harassment includes unwelcome physical contact and advances, demands for sexual favours, showing pornography against a woman's will, and making sexually coloured remarks.
acts_constituting_offence: Unwelcome physical contact and explicit sexual advances
Demand or request for sexual favours
Showing pornography against a woman's will
ai_summary: Defines sexual harassment and prescribes different punishments depending on the nature of the conduct.

title: Assault or Use of Criminal Force to Woman with Intent to Disrobe
chapter: Chapter V - Of Criminal Force and Assault Against Woman
content: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any woman, or abets such act, with the intention of disrobing or compelling her to be naked, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Forcibly disrobing or attempting to make a woman naked is punishable with 3 to 7 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Voyeurism
chapter: Chapter V - Of Criminal Force and Assault Against Woman
content: Watching, capturing images of, or disseminating images of a woman engaging in a private act without lawful consent constitutes voyeurism.
important_points: Private acts include use of lavatory, exposure of private body parts, or private sexual acts.
Consent to capture an image does not imply consent to share it.
Sharing such images without consent is also an offence.
ai_summary: Watching or sharing images of a woman engaged in private acts without consent is a punishable offence.

title: Stalking
chapter: Chapter V - Of Criminal Force and Assault Against Woman
content: A man commits stalking if he repeatedly follows, contacts, or attempts to contact a woman despite clear disinterest, or monitors her internet, email, or electronic communications.
exceptions: Preventing or detecting crime by authorised persons.
Actions required under law.
Conduct that is reasonable and justified in the circumstances.
ai_summary: Repeated unwanted following, contacting, or monitoring of a woman is punishable as stalking.

title: Word, Gesture or Act Intended to Insult Modesty of Woman
chapter: Chapter V - Of Criminal Force and Assault Against Woman
content: Whoever intentionally insults the modesty of a woman through words, sounds, gestures, objects, or intrusion upon her privacy commits an offence.
ai_summary: Insulting a woman's modesty through words, gestures, or invasion of privacy is punishable.

title: Dowry Death
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
content: When a woman's death occurs due to burns, bodily injury, or under abnormal circumstances within seven years of marriage, and she was subjected to cruelty or harassment related to dowry demands shortly before her death, such death is called dowry death.
essential_conditions: Death within 7 years of marriage.
Death by burns, bodily injury, or abnormal circumstances.
Cruelty or harassment by husband or relatives.
Cruelty connected with dowry demand.
Harassment occurred soon before death.
ai_summary: Dowry-related death of a woman within seven years of marriage is punishable with a minimum of seven years imprisonment and may extend to life imprisonment.

title: Cohabitation Caused by Man Deceitfully Inducing Belief of Lawful Marriage
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
content: Every man who deceitfully causes a woman, who is not lawfully married to him, to believe that she is lawfully married to him and thereby induces her to cohabit or have sexual intercourse with him commits an offence.
essential_conditions: Woman is not lawfully married to the accused
False belief of lawful marriage created by the accused
Cohabitation or sexual intercourse based on that belief
ai_summary: Deceiving a woman into believing she is legally married and inducing cohabitation is punishable with up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Marrying Again During Lifetime of Husband or Wife
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
content: A person who marries again during the lifetime of a spouse, where such marriage is void because the first spouse is still alive, commits an offence.
exceptions: Previous marriage declared void by a competent court
Spouse absent for 7 years and not heard of as alive during that period, with disclosure of facts before remarriage
ai_summary: Marrying again while a spouse is alive is punishable, with harsher punishment if the previous marriage was concealed.

title: Marriage Ceremony Fraudulently Gone Through Without Lawful Marriage
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
content: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently goes through a marriage ceremony knowing that no lawful marriage is created commits an offence.
essential_conditions: Marriage ceremony performed
Knowledge that the marriage is not legally valid
Fraudulent or dishonest intention
ai_summary: Fraudulently conducting a marriage ceremony without creating a valid marriage is punishable.

title: Enticing or Taking Away Married Woman
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
content: Whoever takes, entices away, conceals, or detains a married woman with the intention that she may have illicit intercourse with any person commits an offence.
essential_conditions: Woman is married
Accused knows or has reason to believe she is married
Intent of illicit intercourse
ai_summary: Taking or detaining a married woman for illicit intercourse is punishable.

title: Cruelty by Husband or Relatives of Husband
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
content: Whoever, being the husband or a relative of the husband of a woman, subjects her to cruelty commits an offence.
important_note: Cruelty includes conduct likely to drive a woman to suicide, cause grave injury, or harassment related to unlawful demands for property or valuable security.
ai_summary: Cruelty by a husband or his relatives, including harassment and dowry-related abuse, is punishable with up to 3 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Cruelty Defined
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
content: Defines the term 'cruelty' for the purpose of Section 85.

definition.clause_a: Any wilful conduct likely to drive a woman to commit suicide or cause grave injury or danger to her life, limb, mental health, or physical health.
definition.clause_b: Harassment with the intention of coercing a woman or her relatives to meet unlawful demands for property or valuable security, or harassment due to failure to meet such demands.

punishment.reason: Definition section. Punishment provided under Section 85.

ai_summary: Defines cruelty, including conduct causing serious harm and harassment related to unlawful property or dowry demands.

title: Kidnapping, Abducting or Inducing Woman to Compel Her Marriage, etc.
chapter: Chapter V - Of Offences Against Woman
content: Whoever kidnaps, abducts, or induces a woman with intent to compel her marriage against her will or to force or seduce her into illicit intercourse commits an offence.
essential_conditions: Kidnapping or abduction of a woman
Intent to compel marriage against her will
Intent to force or seduce into illicit intercourse
Includes inducement through intimidation, abuse of authority, or compulsion
ai_summary: Kidnapping or inducing a woman for forced marriage or illicit intercourse is punishable with up to ten years imprisonment and fine.

title: Causing Miscarriage
chapter: Chapter V - Of Causing Miscarriage, etc.
content: Whoever voluntarily causes a woman with child to miscarry, except in good faith for saving her life, commits an offence.
important_points: Applies even when the woman causes her own miscarriage.
Exception applies when done in good faith to save the woman's life.
ai_summary: Causing miscarriage without legal justification is punishable, with enhanced punishment when the woman is quick with child.

title: Causing Miscarriage Without Woman's Consent
chapter: Chapter V - Of Causing Miscarriage, etc.
content: Whoever causes miscarriage without the consent of the woman commits an aggravated offence, regardless of whether the woman is quick with child.
ai_summary: Causing miscarriage without the woman's consent is a serious offence punishable with life imprisonment or up to ten years imprisonment and fine.

title: Act Done with Intent to Cause Miscarriage Resulting in Death of Woman
chapter: Chapter V - Of Causing Miscarriage, etc.
content: Whoever, intending to cause miscarriage, performs an act that results in the death of the woman commits an offence. Knowledge that death was likely is not required.
important_points: Intent to cause miscarriage is sufficient.
Knowledge of likelihood of death is not necessary.
Enhanced punishment applies where consent was absent.
ai_summary: Causing the death of a woman while attempting miscarriage is punishable, with life imprisonment possible when done without consent.

title: Act Done with Intent to Prevent Child Being Born Alive or to Cause Death After Birth
chapter: Chapter V - Of Causing Miscarriage, etc.
content: Whoever, before the birth of a child, does any act with the intention of preventing that child from being born alive or causing it to die after birth, and thereby prevents the child from being born alive or causes its death after birth, commits an offence unless the act was done in good faith to save the life of the mother.
exception: Act done in good faith for the purpose of saving the life of the mother.
ai_summary: Intentionally preventing a child from being born alive or causing its death after birth is punishable unless done to save the mother's life.

title: Causing Death of Quick Unborn Child by Act Amounting to Culpable Homicide
chapter: Chapter V - Of Causing Miscarriage, etc.
content: Whoever does an act under circumstances that would amount to culpable homicide if death were caused, and thereby causes the death of a quick unborn child, commits an offence.
illustration: A injures a pregnant woman in circumstances amounting to culpable homicide. The woman survives but the quick unborn child dies. A is guilty under this section.
ai_summary: Causing the death of a quick unborn child through an act amounting to culpable homicide is punishable.

title: Exposure and Abandonment of Child Under Twelve Years
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Against Child
content: Whoever, being the father, mother, or person having care of a child under twelve years of age, exposes or leaves such child in any place with the intention of wholly abandoning the child, commits an offence.
important_note: If the child dies because of abandonment, the offender may additionally be prosecuted for murder or culpable homicide.
ai_summary: Abandoning a child under twelve years with intent to completely desert the child is punishable.

title: Concealment of Birth by Secret Disposal of Dead Body
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Against Child
content: Whoever secretly buries or otherwise disposes of the dead body of a child, whether the child died before, during, or after birth, with the intention of concealing the birth, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Secret disposal of a child's dead body to conceal its birth is punishable.

title: Hiring, Employing or Engaging Child to Commit an Offence
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Against Child
content: Whoever hires, employs, engages, or uses a child for committing an offence commits an offence. Hiring, employing, engaging, or using a child for sexual exploitation or pornography is also covered under this section.
important_points: Applies to all offences committed through a child.
Includes sexual exploitation of children.
Includes use of children for pornography.

punishment.additional_punishment: If the offence is committed, punishment for that offence shall also apply as if committed by the accused himself.

ai_summary: Using a child to commit an offence is punishable with 3 to 10 years imprisonment, and the offender may also receive punishment for the actual offence committed.

title: Procuration of Child
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Against Child
content: Whoever induces any child to go from any place or to do any act with the intention that such child may be, or knowing that it is likely that such child will be, forced or seduced to illicit intercourse with another person commits an offence.
essential_conditions: Victim is a child
Inducement to go from a place or perform an act
Intent or knowledge that the child may be forced or seduced to illicit intercourse
ai_summary: Inducing a child for sexual exploitation or illicit intercourse is punishable with up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Kidnapping or Abducting Child Under Ten Years of Age with Intent to Steal from Its Person
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Against Child
content: Whoever kidnaps or abducts a child under ten years of age with the intention of dishonestly taking movable property from the child commits an offence.
essential_conditions: Child must be under 10 years of age
Kidnapping or abduction
Intent to steal movable property from the child
ai_summary: Kidnapping a child under ten years to steal property from them is punishable with up to 7 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Selling Child for Purposes of Prostitution, etc.
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Against Child
content: Whoever sells, lets to hire, or otherwise disposes of any child for prostitution, illicit intercourse, or any unlawful and immoral purpose commits an offence.
important_presumption: If a female under 18 is sold or hired to a prostitute or brothel keeper, it is presumed she was disposed of for prostitution unless proved otherwise.
ai_summary: Selling or disposing of a child for prostitution or immoral purposes is punishable with up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Buying Child for Purposes of Prostitution, etc.
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Against Child
content: Whoever buys, hires, or obtains possession of a child for prostitution, illicit intercourse, or any unlawful and immoral purpose commits an offence.
important_presumption: A prostitute or brothel manager obtaining possession of a female under 18 is presumed to have done so for prostitution unless proved otherwise.
ai_summary: Buying or obtaining a child for prostitution or immoral purposes is punishable with 7 to 14 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Culpable Homicide
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Whoever causes death by doing an act with the intention of causing death, or causing bodily injury likely to cause death, or with the knowledge that the act is likely to cause death, commits culpable homicide.
important_explanations: Accelerating the death of a diseased or infirm person amounts to causing death.
Death caused by bodily injury remains homicide even if proper treatment could have prevented death.
Death of a child in the womb is not homicide, but causing death of a living child partially born may amount to culpable homicide.
illustrations: Covering a pit to cause a person to fall and die.
Inducing another person to unknowingly shoot and kill someone.
Accidental killing without intention or knowledge does not amount to culpable homicide.

punishment.reason: Definition section. Punishment provided in subsequent sections.

ai_summary: Defines culpable homicide as causing death with intention or knowledge that death is likely to result.

title: Murder
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Except in the cases covered by the statutory exceptions, culpable homicide is murder if the act causing death is done with intention to cause death, intention to cause bodily injury known to be likely to cause death, intention to inflict bodily injury sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death, or with knowledge that the act is so imminently dangerous that it will probably cause death or such bodily injury likely to cause death.
essential_ingredients: Intention to cause bodily injury likely to cause death
Intention to inflict bodily injury sufficient in ordinary course of nature to cause death
Knowledge that the act is imminently dangerous and likely to cause death
exceptions: Grave and sudden provocation
Exceeding right of private defence in good faith
Public servant exceeding powers in good faith
Sudden fight without premeditation
Consent of person above eighteen years
ai_summary: Defines when culpable homicide amounts to murder and specifies five statutory exceptions.

title: Culpable Homicide by Causing Death of Person Other Than Person Whose Death Was Intended
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: When a person commits culpable homicide by causing the death of someone other than the person whose death he intended or knew himself likely to cause, the offence committed is the same as if the intended person had died.
ai_summary: Criminal liability remains the same even if a different person dies instead of the intended victim.

title: Punishment for Murder
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Whoever commits murder shall be punished according to this section.
ai_summary: Murder is punishable with death or imprisonment for life and fine.

title: Punishment for Murder by Life Convict
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: A person serving a sentence of imprisonment for life who commits murder commits an aggravated offence.
ai_summary: A life convict committing murder may be punished with death or imprisonment for the remainder of natural life.

title: Punishment for Culpable Homicide Not Amounting to Murder
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Provides punishment for culpable homicide that does not amount to murder.
ai_summary: Punishment varies depending on whether the act was committed with intention or only knowledge.

title: Causing Death by Negligence
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Whoever causes the death of any person by a rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide commits an offence.
ai_summary: Causing death by negligence is punishable, with enhanced punishment for hit-and-run type cases.

title: Abetment of Suicide of Child or Person of Unsound Mind
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Whoever abets the suicide of a child, person of unsound mind, delirious person, or intoxicated person commits an offence.
ai_summary: Abetting suicide of vulnerable persons is a grave offence punishable up to death penalty.

title: Abetment of Suicide
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Whoever abets the commission of suicide of any person commits an offence.
ai_summary: Abetting the suicide of another person is punishable with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Attempt to Murder
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge and under such circumstances that, if death were caused, the act would amount to murder, commits the offence of attempt to murder.
illustrations: Shooting at a person with intent to kill.
Exposing a child with intent to cause death.
Firing a gun at a person intending murder.
Administering poison with intent to kill.
ai_summary: Attempting to commit murder is punishable even when death does not occur, with enhanced punishment if hurt is caused.

title: Attempt to Commit Culpable Homicide
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge and under such circumstances that, if death were caused, the act would amount to culpable homicide not amounting to murder, commits an offence.
illustration: A, under grave and sudden provocation, fires at Z. If death had occurred, it would amount to culpable homicide not amounting to murder.
ai_summary: Attempting culpable homicide not amounting to murder is punishable, with higher punishment when hurt is caused.

title: Organised Crime
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Continuing unlawful activity carried out by an individual or organised crime syndicate through violence, intimidation, coercion, threat of violence, or other unlawful means for obtaining material or financial benefit constitutes organised crime.

definitions.organised_crime_syndicate: A group of two or more persons acting singly or jointly as a syndicate or gang engaging in continuing unlawful activity.
definitions.continuing_unlawful_activity: A cognizable offence punishable with imprisonment of three years or more where more than one charge-sheet has been filed within the preceding ten years and cognizance has been taken by a competent court.

ai_summary: Defines organised crime and prescribes severe punishments including life imprisonment and death in cases resulting in death.

title: Petty Organised Crime
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: A member of a group or gang committing theft, snatching, cheating, unauthorised ticket selling, gambling, public examination paper selling, or similar criminal acts commits petty organised crime.
ai_summary: Criminal acts committed by gangs or organised groups on a smaller scale constitute petty organised crime.

title: Terrorist Act
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting Life
content: Any act intended to threaten the unity, integrity, sovereignty, security, or economic security of India, or intended to strike terror among people, using explosives, firearms, hazardous substances, kidnapping, coercion, or similar means, constitutes a terrorist act.
ai_summary: Defines terrorist acts and imposes severe punishments including death penalty and life imprisonment.

title: Hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Whoever causes bodily pain, disease, or infirmity to any person is said to cause hurt.
ai_summary: Defines the legal meaning of hurt under BNS.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Whoever causes hurt with the intention of causing hurt, or with knowledge that hurt is likely to be caused, voluntarily causes hurt.
ai_summary: Voluntarily causing hurt is punishable with imprisonment up to one year, fine up to ten thousand rupees, or both.

title: Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: The following kinds of hurt only are designated as grievous hurt.
grievous_hurt_types: Permanent privation of sight of either eye
Permanent privation of hearing of either ear
Privation of any member or joint
Destruction or permanent impairment of powers of any member or joint
Permanent disfiguration of head or face
Fracture or dislocation of bone or tooth
Any hurt endangering life or causing severe bodily pain for fifteen days or inability to follow ordinary pursuits for fifteen days
ai_summary: Defines the eight categories of injuries legally recognised as grievous hurt.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: A person voluntarily causes grievous hurt when he causes grievous hurt and intends or knows himself likely to cause grievous hurt.

group_of_five_or_more_persons.condition: Grievous hurt caused on grounds of race, caste, community, sex, place of birth, language, personal belief, or similar grounds

ai_summary: Voluntarily causing grievous hurt is punishable, with enhanced punishment for permanent disability and group-based attacks.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt or Grievous Hurt by Dangerous Weapons or Means
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Applies where hurt or grievous hurt is caused using dangerous weapons, fire, poison, corrosive substances, explosives, harmful substances, or animals.
ai_summary: Provides enhanced punishment when hurt or grievous hurt is caused through dangerous weapons or harmful means.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt or Grievous Hurt to Extort Property or Constrain to Illegal Act
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Whoever causes hurt or grievous hurt to extort property, valuable security, or to compel a person to do an illegal act commits an offence.
ai_summary: Causing hurt or grievous hurt to extort property or force illegal acts attracts severe punishment.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt or Grievous Hurt to Extort Confession or Compel Restoration of Property
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Whoever causes hurt or grievous hurt to obtain a confession, information, restoration of property, satisfaction of a claim, or related information commits an offence.
illustrations: Police officer torturing a suspect to obtain confession.
Police officer torturing a person to locate stolen property.
Revenue officer torturing a person to recover revenue dues.
ai_summary: Punishes causing hurt or grievous hurt for extracting confessions, information, or recovery of property.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt or Grievous Hurt to Deter Public Servant from Duty
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt or grievous hurt to a public servant while the public servant is discharging official duties, or with intent to prevent or deter such duty, or as retaliation for lawful official acts, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Enhanced punishment applies when hurt or grievous hurt is caused to a public servant performing official duties.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt or Grievous Hurt on Grave and Sudden Provocation
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Where hurt or grievous hurt is caused under grave and sudden provocation, and the offender neither intends nor knows himself likely to cause hurt or grievous hurt to any person other than the person who gave the provocation, this section applies.
important_note: Subject to the same provisos as Exception 1 to Section 101 (Murder).
ai_summary: Provides reduced punishment where hurt or grievous hurt is caused under grave and sudden provocation.

title: Causing Hurt by Means of Poison, etc., with Intent to Commit an Offence
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Whoever administers or causes another person to take poison, stupefying, intoxicating, unwholesome drugs, or similar substances with intent to cause hurt, facilitate an offence, or knowing that hurt is likely to result, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Administering poison or harmful substances to facilitate offences or cause hurt is punishable with up to ten years imprisonment.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt by Use of Acid, etc.
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Whoever causes permanent or partial damage, deformity, burns, maiming, disfigurement, disability, grievous hurt, or a permanent vegetative state by throwing acid, administering acid, or using similar means commits an offence.

definitions.acid: Any acidic, corrosive, or burning substance capable of causing bodily injury, scars, disfigurement, or disability.

ai_summary: Acid attacks carry a minimum punishment of ten years imprisonment and may extend to life imprisonment.

title: Act Endangering Life or Personal Safety of Others
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Hurt
content: Whoever acts rashly or negligently in a manner that endangers human life or personal safety commits an offence.
ai_summary: Punishes rash or negligent acts endangering life, with enhanced punishment when hurt or grievous hurt results.

title: Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
content: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed is said to wrongfully restrain that person.
exception: Obstruction of a private way over land or water by a person who in good faith believes he has a lawful right to obstruct it is not an offence.
illustration: A obstructs a path along which Z has a legal right to pass and prevents Z from proceeding. A wrongfully restrains Z.
ai_summary: Preventing a person from moving in a direction where they have a legal right to proceed constitutes wrongful restraint.

title: Wrongful Confinement
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
content: Whoever wrongfully restrains a person in such a manner as to prevent that person from proceeding beyond certain circumscribing limits is said to wrongfully confine that person.
illustrations: Locking a person inside a room.
Placing armed guards to prevent a person from leaving a building.
ai_summary: Wrongful confinement includes illegally restricting a person's movement within defined limits, with enhanced punishment in aggravated circumstances.

title: Force
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
content: A person uses force when he causes motion, change of motion, or cessation of motion to another person, or to any substance that comes into contact with that person's body, clothing, or belongings, affecting the person's sense of feeling.
methods_of_force: By arranging substances to move without further action
By inducing an animal to move, change motion, or stop
ai_summary: Defines the legal concept of force and the methods through which force may be applied.

title: Criminal Force
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
content: Whoever intentionally uses force against another person without consent in order to commit an offence, or intending or knowing it likely to cause injury, fear, or annoyance, uses criminal force.
essential_ingredients: Purpose of committing an offence or causing injury, fear, or annoyance
illustrations: Unfastening a boat causing it to drift with a passenger.
Driving horses to frighten a passenger.
Stopping a palanquin to commit robbery.
Pushing a person intentionally.
Throwing objects to strike or frighten someone.
Pulling a woman's veil.
Pouring boiling water into a bath being used by another.
Inciting a dog to attack a person.
ai_summary: Defines criminal force as intentional use of force without consent to commit an offence or cause injury, fear, or annoyance.

title: Assault
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
content: Whoever makes a gesture or preparation intending or knowing it likely that it will cause another person to apprehend the imminent use of criminal force commits assault.
important_note: Mere words do not amount to assault, but words may give meaning to gestures or preparations and thereby constitute assault.
illustrations: Shaking a fist at another person to make them believe they are about to be struck.
Unfastening the muzzle of a ferocious dog to threaten another person.
Raising a stick and threatening to beat someone.
ai_summary: Assault occurs when a person creates a reasonable apprehension of imminent criminal force through gestures or preparations.

title: Assault or Criminal Force Otherwise Than on Grave and Sudden Provocation
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
content: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person otherwise than on grave and sudden provocation given by that person commits an offence.

provocation_not_valid_if: Provocation was intentionally sought or voluntarily provoked by the offender.
Provocation arose from an act done in obedience to law.
Provocation arose from a public servant lawfully exercising official powers.
Provocation arose from lawful exercise of the right of private defence.
explanations.question_of_fact: Whether the provocation was grave and sudden is a question of fact.

ai_summary: Punishes assault or criminal force where the act is not justified by grave and sudden provocation.

title: Assault or Criminal Force to Deter Public Servant from Discharge of Duty
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
content: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force against a public servant while the public servant is performing official duties, or with intent to prevent or deter such duty, or in consequence of lawful official acts, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Public servant is performing official duty or has performed lawful official duty
Assault or criminal force used intentionally
ai_summary: Protects public servants from assault or criminal force connected with the discharge of official duties.

title: Assault or Criminal Force with Intent to Dishonour Person
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
content: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force against any person with the intention of dishonouring that person, otherwise than on grave and sudden provocation, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Absence of legally recognised grave and sudden provocation
ai_summary: Punishes assault or criminal force committed with the intention of insulting or dishonouring another person.

title: Assault or Criminal Force in Attempt to Commit Theft of Property Carried by a Person
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
content: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person while attempting to commit theft of property being worn or carried by that person commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Property is being worn or carried by the victim
Assault or criminal force used during the attempt
ai_summary: Punishes use of assault or criminal force while attempting to steal property from a person.

title: Assault or Criminal Force in Attempt to Wrongfully Confine a Person
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
content: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force against any person while attempting to wrongfully confine that person commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Victim is the person sought to be confined
ai_summary: Punishes assault or criminal force used in an attempt to wrongfully confine another person.

title: Assault or Criminal Force on Grave and Sudden Provocation
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
content: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person on grave and sudden provocation given by that person commits an offence.
note: Subject to the same explanations and limitations applicable under Section 131 regarding grave and sudden provocation.
ai_summary: Provides reduced punishment for assault or criminal force committed under grave and sudden provocation.

title: Kidnapping
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Kidnapping is of two kinds: kidnapping from India and kidnapping from lawful guardianship.

types.kidnapping_from_india: Conveying any person beyond the limits of India without valid consent.
types.kidnapping_from_lawful_guardianship: Taking or enticing a child or person of unsound mind out of the keeping of a lawful guardian without consent.

exception: Does not apply to a person who in good faith believes himself to be the father of an illegitimate child or entitled to lawful custody, unless done for an immoral or unlawful purpose.
ai_summary: Defines kidnapping and prescribes punishment up to seven years imprisonment and fine.

title: Abduction
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Whoever by force compels, or by deceitful means induces, any person to go from any place is said to abduct that person.
essential_ingredients: Use of force or deceitful means
Movement of a person from one place to another
ai_summary: Defines abduction as compelling or inducing a person by force or deceit to move from a place.

title: Kidnapping or Maiming a Child for Purposes of Begging
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Whoever kidnaps, obtains custody of, or maims a child for the purpose of begging commits a serious offence.

begging: Soliciting or receiving alms in public places
Entering private premises to seek alms
Displaying injuries, deformities, or diseases to obtain alms
Using a child as an exhibit for soliciting alms

ai_summary: Provides severe punishment for kidnapping or maiming children for begging purposes.

title: Kidnapping or Abduction for Murder, Ransom, Secret Confinement, Slavery or Grievous Harm
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Punishes kidnapping or abduction committed for murder, ransom, secret confinement, slavery, grievous hurt, or similar serious purposes.
ai_summary: Provides enhanced punishments for kidnapping or abduction connected with murder, ransom, slavery, grievous hurt, or secret confinement.

title: Importation of Girl or Boy from Foreign Country
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Whoever imports into India any girl under twenty-one years of age or any boy under eighteen years of age from a foreign country with the intention or knowledge that such girl or boy may be forced or seduced to illicit intercourse commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Importation into India from outside India
Girl below 21 years or boy below 18 years
Intent or knowledge of sexual exploitation or illicit intercourse
ai_summary: Importing a minor into India for sexual exploitation or illicit intercourse is punishable with up to ten years imprisonment and fine.

title: Wrongfully Concealing or Keeping in Confinement Kidnapped or Abducted Person
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Whoever knowingly conceals or wrongfully confines a person who has been kidnapped or abducted commits an offence.
special_rule: Punishment shall be the same as if the offender himself had committed the kidnapping or abduction with the same intention, knowledge, or purpose.
ai_summary: A person who knowingly conceals or confines a kidnapped or abducted person receives the same punishment as the kidnapper or abductor.

title: Trafficking of Person
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Recruiting, transporting, harbouring, transferring, or receiving a person for exploitation through threats, force, coercion, abduction, fraud, deception, abuse of power, or inducement constitutes trafficking.
important_points: Victim consent is immaterial.
Exploitation includes sexual exploitation, physical exploitation, slavery, servitude, beggary, and forced organ removal.
ai_summary: Defines human trafficking and prescribes severe punishments, including life imprisonment in aggravated cases.

title: Exploitation of a Trafficked Person
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Whoever knowingly engages a trafficked child or trafficked person for sexual exploitation commits an offence.
ai_summary: Knowingly exploiting a trafficked child or trafficked person for sexual purposes is punishable.

title: Habitual Dealing in Slaves
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Whoever habitually imports, exports, removes, buys, sells, traffics, or deals in slaves commits an offence.
ai_summary: Habitual involvement in slave trading or dealing in slaves is punishable with life imprisonment or up to ten years imprisonment and fine.

title: Unlawful Compulsory Labour
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
content: Whoever unlawfully compels any person to labour against the will of that person commits the offence of unlawful compulsory labour.
essential_ingredients: Labour performed against the person's will
ai_summary: Compelling a person to work against their will is punishable with imprisonment up to one year, fine, or both.

title: Waging, Attempting to Wage, or Abetting War Against Government of India
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever wages war against the Government of India, attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war commits an offence.
illustration: Joining an insurrection against the Government of India amounts to this offence.
ai_summary: Waging, attempting to wage, or abetting war against the Government of India is punishable with death or life imprisonment and fine.

title: Conspiracy to Commit Offences Punishable Under Section 147
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever conspires to wage war against the Government of India or conspires to overawe the Central Government or any State Government through criminal force or the show of criminal force commits an offence.
important_note: To constitute conspiracy under this section, no overt act or illegal omission is necessary.
ai_summary: Conspiracy to wage war or overawe the Government by criminal force is punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Collecting Men, Arms or Ammunition with Intention of Waging War
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever collects men, arms, ammunition, or otherwise prepares to wage war against the Government of India with the intention of waging war commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Collection of men, arms, or ammunition
Intention to wage war against Government of India
ai_summary: Preparing to wage war against the Government of India by collecting men, arms, or ammunition is punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Concealing Design to Wage War
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
