content: Whoever intentionally conceals the existence of a design to wage war against the Government of India, knowing that such concealment is likely to facilitate the waging of war, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Existence of a design to wage war
Knowledge or intention that concealment will facilitate war
ai_summary: Concealing a plan to wage war against the Government of India is punishable with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Assaulting President, Governor, etc., with Intent to Compel or Restrain Exercise of Lawful Power
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever, with the intention of inducing or compelling the President of India or the Governor of any State to exercise or refrain from exercising any lawful power, assaults, wrongfully restrains, attempts to wrongfully restrain, overawes by criminal force or show of criminal force, or attempts to overawe such President or Governor, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Victim is the President of India or Governor of a State
Assault, wrongful restraint, or criminal force
Intent to compel or restrain exercise of lawful powers
ai_summary: Using force, restraint, or intimidation against the President or Governor to influence their lawful powers is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.

title: Act Endangering Sovereignty, Unity and Integrity of India
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever purposely or knowingly, by spoken or written words, signs, visible representations, electronic communication, financial means, or otherwise, excites or attempts to excite secession, armed rebellion, subversive activities, separatist feelings, or endangers the sovereignty, unity, and integrity of India, commits an offence.
important_explanation: Criticism or disapproval of Government measures, administrative actions, or policies expressed through lawful means for seeking change does not constitute an offence unless it incites secession, armed rebellion, or activities prohibited under this section.
ai_summary: Acts promoting secession, armed rebellion, or activities endangering India's sovereignty, unity, and integrity are punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to seven years and fine.

title: Waging War Against Foreign State at Peace with Government of India
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever wages war against a foreign State that is at peace with the Government of India, attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Waging war against a foreign State that is at peace with India is punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to seven years and fine.

title: Committing Depredation on Territories of Foreign State at Peace with Government of India
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever commits depredation or prepares to commit depredation on the territory of a foreign State that is at peace with the Government of India commits an offence.
additional_consequence: Property used, intended to be used, or acquired through such depredation is liable to forfeiture.
ai_summary: Committing or preparing to commit depredation against a friendly foreign State is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years, fine, and forfeiture of property.

title: Receiving Property Taken by War or Depredation
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever receives property knowing that it was obtained through offences under Sections 153 or 154 commits an offence.
ai_summary: Knowingly receiving property obtained through war-related or depredation offences is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years, fine, and forfeiture of the property.

title: Public Servant Allowing State Prisoner or Prisoner of War to Escape
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever, being a public servant having custody of a State prisoner or prisoner of war, voluntarily allows such prisoner to escape from lawful confinement commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Custody of State prisoner or prisoner of war
Voluntary permission or facilitation of escape
ai_summary: A public servant who intentionally allows a State prisoner or prisoner of war to escape is punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Public Servant Negligently Suffering State Prisoner or Prisoner of War to Escape
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever, being a public servant having custody of a State prisoner or prisoner of war, negligently allows such prisoner to escape from lawful confinement commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Custody of State prisoner or prisoner of war
ai_summary: A public servant whose negligence results in the escape of a State prisoner or prisoner of war is punishable with simple imprisonment up to three years and fine.

title: Aiding Escape of State Prisoner or Prisoner of War
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
content: Whoever knowingly aids, assists, rescues, attempts to rescue, harbours, conceals, or resists the recapture of a State prisoner or prisoner of war who has escaped lawful custody commits an offence.
explanation: A State prisoner or prisoner of war permitted to remain at large on parole within specified limits is deemed to have escaped lawful custody if he goes beyond those limits.
ai_summary: Helping, hiding, rescuing, or assisting the escape of a State prisoner or prisoner of war is punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Abetting Mutiny or Attempting to Seduce Military Personnel from Duty
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: Whoever abets the committing of mutiny by an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman of the Army, Navy, or Air Force of the Government of India, or attempts to seduce such personnel from allegiance or duty, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Attempt to seduce military personnel from allegiance
Attempt to seduce military personnel from duty
ai_summary: Abetting mutiny or inducing military personnel to abandon allegiance or duty is punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Abetment of Mutiny if Mutiny is Committed
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: Whoever abets mutiny by an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman of the Army, Navy, or Air Force, and such mutiny is actually committed as a consequence of that abetment, commits an aggravated offence.
essential_ingredients: Mutiny committed as a consequence of the abetment
ai_summary: If mutiny occurs because of the accused's abetment, punishment may extend to death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to ten years with fine.

title: Abetment of Assault on Superior Officer
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: Whoever abets an assault by an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman of the Army, Navy, or Air Force of the Government of India upon a superior officer while such superior officer is acting in the execution of his office commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Superior officer is acting in execution of official duty
ai_summary: Abetting an assault by military personnel on a superior officer performing official duties is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and fine.

title: Abetment of Assault on Superior Officer if Assault is Committed
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: Whoever abets an assault by an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman upon a superior officer, and such assault is actually committed as a consequence of the abetment, commits an aggravated offence.
essential_ingredients: Assault committed due to the abetment
ai_summary: Where assault on a superior officer is actually committed due to abetment, punishment extends up to seven years and fine.

title: Abetment of Desertion
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: Whoever abets the desertion of any officer, soldier, sailor, or airman of the Army, Navy, or Air Force of the Government of India commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Army, Navy, or Air Force personnel involved
ai_summary: Abetting desertion of military personnel is punishable with imprisonment up to two years, fine, or both.

title: Harbouring Deserter
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman has deserted, harbours such deserter commits an offence.
exception: The section does not apply where the harbour is given by the spouse of the deserter.
essential_ingredients: Knowledge or reason to believe that the person is a deserter
Deserter belongs to Army, Navy, or Air Force
ai_summary: Knowingly harbouring a military deserter is punishable with imprisonment up to two years, fine, or both, except where harbour is provided by the spouse.

title: Neglect by Master of Merchant Vessel in Respect of Deserter
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: The master or person in charge of a merchant vessel on which a military deserter is concealed is liable for neglect if the concealment could have been discovered through proper diligence, discipline, or performance of duty.
essential_ingredients: Master or person in charge failed in duty
Concealment could have been discovered through reasonable care
ai_summary: A vessel master who negligently fails to discover a concealed military deserter may be fined up to three thousand rupees.

title: Abetment of Act of Insubordination by Soldier, Sailor or Airman
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: Whoever abets what he knows to be an act of insubordination by an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman of the Army, Navy, or Air Force of the Government of India commits an offence if such act of insubordination is committed in consequence of that abetment.
essential_ingredients: Actual commission of insubordination due to the abetment
ai_summary: Abetting an act of military insubordination is punishable when the act is actually committed as a result of the abetment.

title: Persons Subject to Certain Acts
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: Persons subject to the Air Force Act, 1950, Army Act, 1950, or Navy Act, 1957 shall not be punished under this Sanhita for offences defined in this Chapter.
important_note: This section excludes military personnel governed by special military laws from punishment under this Chapter of BNS.
ai_summary: Military personnel governed by special service laws are excluded from punishment under this Chapter of BNS.

title: Wearing Garb or Carrying Token Used by Soldier, Sailor or Airman
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
content: Whoever, not being a soldier, sailor, or airman of the Government of India, wears any garb or carries any token resembling that used by such personnel with the intention of making others believe that he is such a member of the armed forces commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Use of military uniform, insignia, or token
Intent to deceive others into believing he is military personnel
ai_summary: Impersonating military personnel by wearing military garb or carrying military tokens is punishable.

title: Candidate and Electoral Right Defined
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
content: Defines the terms 'candidate' and 'electoral right' for the purposes of election offences under this Chapter.

definitions.candidate: A person who has been nominated as a candidate at any election.
definitions.electoral_right: The right of a person to stand, or not stand, as a candidate, to withdraw candidature, to vote, or to refrain from voting at an election.

ai_summary: Provides definitions of candidate and electoral right used throughout election-related offences.

title: Bribery
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
content: Whoever gives gratification to induce a person to exercise an electoral right, or rewards a person for exercising such right, or accepts gratification for exercising or inducing another to exercise such right, commits the offence of bribery.
important_proviso: A declaration of public policy or a promise of public action is not an offence under this section.
deemed_actions: Offering or agreeing to give gratification is deemed giving gratification.
Obtaining, agreeing to accept, or attempting to obtain gratification is deemed accepting gratification.
Accepting gratification for an act not intended to be done is also deemed acceptance.
ai_summary: Defines electoral bribery involving giving or accepting gratification to influence electoral rights.

title: Undue Influence at Elections
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
content: Whoever voluntarily interferes or attempts to interfere with the free exercise of any electoral right commits the offence of undue influence at an election.

deemed_undue_influence.description: Threatening any candidate, voter, or any person in whom a candidate or voter is interested with injury of any kind.
Inducing or attempting to induce a candidate or voter to believe that he or any person in whom he is interested will become an object of Divine displeasure or spiritual censure.

exceptions: Declaration of public policy.
Promise of public action.
Mere exercise of a legal right without intent to interfere with an electoral right.
ai_summary: Defines undue influence at elections as interference with the free exercise of electoral rights through threats, coercion, or improper influence.

title: Personation at Elections
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
content: Whoever applies for a voting paper or votes in the name of another person, whether living, dead, or fictitious, or votes more than once at the same election, commits the offence of personation at an election.
covered_acts: Voting in the name of another living person.
Voting in the name of a deceased person.
Voting more than once in the same election.
exception: Does not apply to a person legally authorised to vote as a proxy for an elector under applicable law.
ai_summary: Defines personation at elections, including voting in another person's name or voting multiple times.

title: Punishment for Bribery
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
content: Provides punishment for the offence of bribery defined under Section 170.
definition_of_treating: A form of bribery where the gratification consists of food, drink, entertainment, or provision.
ai_summary: Electoral bribery is punishable with imprisonment up to one year, fine, or both. Bribery by treating is punishable with fine only.

title: Punishment for Undue Influence or Personation at Elections
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
content: Provides punishment for the offences of undue influence and personation at elections.
ai_summary: Undue influence and personation at elections are punishable with imprisonment up to one year, fine, or both.

title: False Statement in Connection with an Election
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
content: Whoever, with intent to affect the result of an election, makes or publishes a false statement of fact relating to the personal character or conduct of a candidate, knowing it to be false or not believing it to be true, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Statement relates to personal character or conduct of a candidate.
Accused knows it is false or does not believe it to be true.
ai_summary: Publishing false statements about a candidate's character or conduct to influence election results is punishable with fine.

title: Illegal Payments in Connection with an Election
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
content: Whoever, without the general or special written authority of a candidate, incurs or authorises expenses for public meetings, advertisements, circulars, publications, or any other activity intended to promote or procure the election of such candidate commits an offence.
proviso: If expenses not exceeding ten rupees are incurred without authority and written approval of the candidate is obtained within ten days, the expenses shall be deemed authorised.
ai_summary: Incurring election expenses without written authority from a candidate is punishable with a fine up to ten thousand rupees.

title: Failure to Keep Election Expense Accounts
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
content: Whoever is legally required to maintain accounts of election expenses and fails to keep such accounts commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Legal duty to maintain election accounts
ai_summary: Failure to maintain legally required election expense accounts is punishable with a fine up to five thousand rupees.

title: Counterfeiting Coin, Government Stamp, Currency-Note or Bank-Note
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting, any coin, Government stamp, currency-note, or bank-note commits an offence.

definitions.bank_note: A promissory note or engagement for payment of money to bearer on demand issued by a banking institution or sovereign authority and intended to function as money.
definitions.coin: Has the meaning assigned under the Coinage Act, 2011 and includes metal lawfully used as money.
definitions.counterfeiting_government_stamp: Making a genuine stamp of one denomination appear to be a genuine stamp of another denomination.
definitions.counterfeiting_coin: Making a genuine coin appear to be a different coin with intent to deceive.

ai_summary: Counterfeiting coins, Government stamps, currency-notes, or bank-notes is punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Using, Trafficking, Importing or Exporting Forged or Counterfeit Coin, Stamp, Currency-Note or Bank-Note
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever imports, exports, sells, delivers, buys, receives, traffics, or uses as genuine any forged or counterfeit coin, Government stamp, currency-note, or bank-note knowing or having reason to believe it to be forged or counterfeit commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Knowledge or reason to believe it is counterfeit
Import, export, sale, purchase, delivery, trafficking, or use as genuine
ai_summary: Knowingly dealing in or using forged or counterfeit currency, stamps, or coins is punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Possession of Forged or Counterfeit Coin, Stamp, Currency-Note or Bank-Note
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever possesses any forged or counterfeit coin, Government stamp, currency-note, or bank-note knowing or having reason to believe it to be forged or counterfeit and intending to use it as genuine, or allow it to be used as genuine, commits an offence.
explanation: Possession from a lawful source, if established by the person, does not constitute an offence under this section.
essential_ingredients: Knowledge or reason to believe it is counterfeit
Intention to use as genuine or permit use as genuine
ai_summary: Possessing counterfeit currency, coins, or stamps with intent to use them as genuine is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years, fine, or both.

title: Instruments or Materials for Forging or Counterfeiting Coin, Stamp, Currency-Note or Bank-Note
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever makes, mends, performs any part of the process of making or mending, buys, sells, disposes of, or possesses any machinery, die, instrument, or material intended for use in forging or counterfeiting any coin, Government revenue stamp, currency-note, or bank-note commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Knowledge or reason to believe that the equipment will be used for counterfeiting
ai_summary: Possessing, making, repairing, buying, or selling equipment intended for counterfeiting currency, coins, or Government stamps is punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Documents Resembling Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever makes, causes to be made, uses, or delivers any document resembling a currency-note or bank-note in a manner likely to deceive commits an offence.

182(1).offence: Making, using, or delivering deceptive documents resembling currency-notes or bank-notes.

182(2).offence: Refusing without lawful excuse to disclose the printer or maker of such document when required by a police officer.

182(3).rule: Where a person's name appears on such document, it may be presumed until the contrary is proved that the person caused it to be made.

ai_summary: Creating or circulating documents resembling currency-notes or bank-notes is punishable with fine, and refusal to disclose their source attracts additional penalty.

title: Removing Writing from Revenue Stamp or Removing Revenue Stamp from Document
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to the Government removes writing from a stamped document or removes a used revenue stamp from a document so that it may be reused commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Fraudulent intention or intent to cause Government loss
Removal of writing from stamped document
Removal of used revenue stamp for reuse
ai_summary: Fraudulently removing writing or stamps to enable reuse of Government revenue stamps is punishable with imprisonment up to three years, fine, or both.

title: Using Previously Used Revenue Stamp
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to the Government uses a Government revenue stamp which he knows has already been used commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Use of previously used revenue stamp
Knowledge that stamp has already been used
Fraudulent intent or intent to cause Government loss
ai_summary: Using a revenue stamp known to have been previously used is punishable with imprisonment up to two years, fine, or both.

title: Erasing Marks from Used Revenue Stamps or Dealing in Such Stamps
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to the Government erases or removes cancellation marks from a revenue stamp, or knowingly possesses, sells, or disposes of such altered or previously used stamp commits an offence.
covered_acts: Removing cancellation marks from used stamp
ai_summary: Removing cancellation marks from revenue stamps or dealing in such altered stamps is punishable with imprisonment up to three years, fine, or both.

title: Prohibition of Fictitious Stamps
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever makes, knowingly utters, deals in, sells, uses for postal purposes, possesses without lawful excuse, or possesses instruments or materials for making any fictitious stamp commits an offence.

definition.fictitious_stamp: A stamp falsely purporting to be issued by Government for denoting postage, including any imitation, facsimile, or representation of such stamp.

covered_acts: Possessing fictitious stamps without lawful excuse
Possessing dies, plates, instruments or materials for making fictitious stamps
ai_summary: Making, possessing, selling, or using fictitious postage stamps is punishable with fine and the materials may be seized and forfeited.

title: Person Employed in Mint Causing Coin to be of Different Weight or Composition
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever, being employed in a lawfully established mint in India, intentionally causes a coin issued from that mint to differ in weight or composition from that fixed by law commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Coin differs from legally prescribed weight or composition
ai_summary: Mint employees who intentionally alter the lawful weight or composition of coins are punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.

title: Unlawfully Taking Coining Instruments from Mint
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes and Government Stamps
content: Whoever, without lawful authority, removes any coining tool or instrument from a lawfully established mint in India commits an offence.
ai_summary: Removing coining instruments from a mint without authority is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.

title: Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against Public Tranquillity
content: An assembly of five or more persons is designated as an unlawful assembly if its common object falls within the categories specified by law.
common_objects: To overawe Government, Legislature, or public servants by criminal force
To resist execution of law or legal process
To commit mischief, criminal trespass, or any offence
To obtain possession of property by criminal force
To compel a person to do what he is not legally bound to do or omit what he is legally entitled to do
important_explanation: An assembly which was lawful when formed may subsequently become an unlawful assembly.
special_rule: A person who hires, engages, employs, promotes, or connives in employing persons for an unlawful assembly is punishable as if he were a member of the assembly and liable for offences committed in pursuance of such employment.
ai_summary: Defines unlawful assembly and prescribes punishments for membership, armed participation, hiring members, and disobeying dispersal orders.

title: Liability of Members of Unlawful Assembly for Offences Committed in Pursuance of Common Object
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against Public Tranquillity
content: If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members knew to be likely to be committed, every member of that assembly is guilty of that offence.
legal_principle: Constructive or vicarious criminal liability of members of an unlawful assembly.
punishment_reference: Punishment depends on the underlying offence committed.
ai_summary: Every member of an unlawful assembly may be held liable for offences committed in furtherance of the assembly's common object.

title: Rioting
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against Public Tranquillity
content: Whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, every member of that assembly is guilty of the offence of rioting.
essential_ingredients: Act committed in prosecution of the common object
ai_summary: When force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, every member becomes guilty of rioting. Armed rioting carries higher punishment.

title: Wantonly Giving Provocation with Intent to Cause Riot
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against Public Tranquillity
content: Whoever illegally and maliciously gives provocation intending or knowing it is likely to cause rioting commits an offence.
ai_summary: Provoking others with the intention of causing a riot is punishable, with greater punishment if the riot actually occurs.

title: Liability of Owners, Occupiers, Agents or Managers for Unlawful Assemblies and Riots
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against Public Tranquillity
content: Owners, occupiers, agents, managers, or beneficiaries of land where unlawful assemblies or riots occur may be punished if they fail to take lawful steps to prevent, suppress, disperse, or report such activities.

sub_sections.193(1): Failure to notify police or take lawful measures to prevent, suppress, or disperse unlawful assemblies or riots.
sub_sections.193(2): Liability of owners, occupiers, or beneficiaries who fail to prevent or suppress riots committed for their benefit.
sub_sections.193(3): Liability of agents or managers who fail to prevent or suppress such riots.

ai_summary: Owners, occupiers, agents, or beneficiaries may be fined for failing to prevent, report, suppress, or disperse unlawful assemblies or riots.

title: Affray
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against Public Tranquillity
content: When two or more persons fight in a public place and thereby disturb the public peace, they are said to commit an affray.
ai_summary: Fighting in a public place resulting in disturbance of public peace constitutes affray.

title: Assaulting or Obstructing Public Servant While Dispersing Unlawful Assembly, Riot or Affray
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against Public Tranquillity
content: Whoever assaults, obstructs, threatens, or uses criminal force against a public servant while the public servant is dispersing an unlawful assembly or suppressing a riot or affray commits an offence.
ai_summary: Assaulting or obstructing a public servant while controlling riots or unlawful assemblies attracts imprisonment and significant fines.

title: Promoting Enmity Between Different Groups and Acts Prejudicial to Harmony
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against Public Tranquillity
content: Whoever promotes or attempts to promote disharmony, enmity, hatred, or ill-will between different groups on grounds such as religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, caste, community, or any other ground, or commits acts prejudicial to harmony and public tranquillity, commits an offence.
covered_acts: Organising or participating in activities training persons to use criminal force or violence against groups
ai_summary: Promoting enmity, hatred, or violence between communities is punishable, with enhanced punishment when committed in places of worship.

title: Imputations and Assertions Prejudicial to National Integration
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against Public Tranquillity
content: Whoever makes or publishes imputations, assertions, appeals, false information, or statements that promote disharmony, deny rights to groups, question allegiance to the Constitution, or jeopardise the sovereignty, unity, integrity, or security of India commits an offence.
covered_acts: Questioning allegiance of communities to the Constitution
Advocating denial of rights to communities
Publishing statements causing hatred or enmity
Publishing false or misleading information affecting sovereignty, unity, integrity, or security of India
ai_summary: Publishing statements or false information that threaten national integration, constitutional allegiance, or India's sovereignty is punishable.

title: Public Servant Disobeying Law with Intent to Cause Injury
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
content: Whoever, being a public servant, knowingly disobeys any legal direction regulating the manner in which he must conduct himself as a public servant, intending to cause injury or knowing that such disobedience is likely to cause injury to any person, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Intent or knowledge of likely injury
ai_summary: A public servant who knowingly disobeys the law and causes or is likely to cause injury is punishable.

title: Public Servant Disobeying Direction Under Law Regarding Investigation
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
content: Whoever, being a public servant, knowingly violates legal directions relating to criminal investigations, including unlawfully requiring attendance, conducting investigations improperly, or failing to record information regarding specified cognizable offences, commits an offence.
covered_acts: Failure to record information regarding specified cognizable offences
important_note: Includes failure to record information relating to serious offences such as rape, sexual offences, trafficking, acid attack, and related crimes specified in the section.
ai_summary: Public servants who knowingly violate investigation procedures or fail to record information regarding specified serious offences are punishable.

title: Hospital Authorities Contravening Legal Duty
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
content: Whoever, being in charge of a public or private hospital, contravenes the provisions of Section 397 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, commits an offence.
applicable_to: Hospitals run by any other person
ai_summary: Hospital authorities who violate duties imposed under BNSS Section 397 are punishable with imprisonment, fine, or both.

title: Public Servant Framing Incorrect Document or Electronic Record
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
content: Whoever, being a public servant charged with the preparation or translation of any document or electronic record, knowingly prepares, frames, or translates it incorrectly with intent to cause injury or knowing that injury is likely to be caused, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Responsible for preparing or translating a document or electronic record
Knowingly prepares or translates it incorrectly
Intent to cause injury or knowledge of likely injury
ai_summary: A public servant who knowingly prepares or translates an incorrect document or electronic record to cause injury is punishable.

title: Public Servant Unlawfully Engaging in Trade
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
content: Whoever, being a public servant legally prohibited from engaging in trade, engages in trade commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Legal prohibition against engaging in trade
ai_summary: A public servant who unlawfully engages in trade may be punished with imprisonment, fine, both, or community service.

title: Public Servant Unlawfully Purchasing Property
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
content: Whoever, being a public servant legally prohibited from purchasing or bidding for certain property, purchases or bids for such property directly or indirectly commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Legal prohibition on purchasing or bidding
Purchase or bid made directly or through another person
ai_summary: A public servant unlawfully purchasing or bidding for restricted property is punishable, and the property may be confiscated.

title: Personation of Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
content: Whoever falsely pretends to hold a public office, or impersonates another public servant, and performs or attempts to perform any act under colour of such office, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Or impersonation of an actual public servant
Performance or attempted performance of an official act
ai_summary: Impersonating a public servant and acting under that false authority is punishable with imprisonment from six months to three years and fine.

title: Wearing Garb or Carrying Token Used by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
content: Whoever does not belong to a particular class of public servants but wears their uniform, garb, or carries their token with intent to make others believe that he belongs to that class commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Person is not a member of the concerned class of public servants
Use of official uniform, garb, badge, or token
Intent or knowledge that others may believe he is a public servant
ai_summary: Wearing the uniform or carrying the token of a public servant with intent to deceive is punishable.

title: Absconding to Avoid Service of Summons or Other Proceeding
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever absconds in order to avoid being served with a summons, notice, or order issued by a legally competent public servant commits an offence.
ai_summary: Absconding to avoid service of a summons, notice, or order is punishable, with enhanced punishment when related to court proceedings.

title: Preventing Service or Publication of Summons, Notice, Order or Proclamation
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever intentionally prevents service, affixing, publication, or lawful proclamation of any summons, notice, order, or proclamation issued by a competent public servant commits an offence.
ai_summary: Preventing service or publication of legal notices, summons, or proclamations is punishable.

title: Non-Attendance in Obedience to an Order from Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever, being legally bound to attend at a specified place and time in obedience to a summons, notice, order, or proclamation, intentionally fails to attend or leaves before being lawfully permitted commits an offence.
illustrations: Failure to appear before a High Court under subpoena.
Failure to appear before a District Judge as a summoned witness.
ai_summary: Failure to attend when legally required by summons, notice, order, or proclamation is punishable.

title: Non-Appearance in Response to a Proclamation Under Section 84 BNSS
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever fails to appear at the specified place and time as required by a proclamation issued under Section 84(1) of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 commits an offence.
ai_summary: Failure to appear in response to a proclamation is punishable, with enhanced punishment for proclaimed offenders.

title: Omission to Produce Document or Electronic Record
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever, being legally bound to produce or deliver a document or electronic record to a public servant, intentionally fails to do so commits an offence.
illustration: A person legally required to produce a document before a District Court intentionally fails to produce it.
ai_summary: Failure to produce a legally required document or electronic record is punishable, with higher punishment when production is required before a court.

title: Omission to Give Notice or Information to Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever, being legally bound to give any notice or furnish information to a public servant, intentionally omits to do so in the manner and time required by law commits an offence.

211(a).offence: General omission to give notice or information.

211(b).offence: Omission relating to commission, prevention, or detection of an offence, or apprehension of an offender.

211(c).offence: Omission of notice or information required under Section 394 of Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023.

ai_summary: Failure to provide legally required information to a public servant is punishable, with enhanced punishment when related to criminal offences.

title: Furnishing False Information
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever, being legally bound to furnish information to a public servant, knowingly provides false information or information which he has reason to believe is false commits an offence.

212(b).offence: False information relating to commission, prevention, or detection of an offence or apprehension of an offender.

important_note: Enhanced punishment applies when the false information relates to criminal offences or offenders.
ai_summary: Knowingly furnishing false information to a public servant is punishable, especially when related to criminal investigations.

title: Refusing Oath or Affirmation
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever refuses to bind himself by oath or affirmation to state the truth when legally required by a competent public servant commits an offence.
ai_summary: Refusing to take a legally required oath or affirmation is punishable.

title: Refusing to Answer Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever, being legally bound to state the truth to a public servant, refuses to answer a lawful question asked by such public servant commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Question asked by competent public servant
ai_summary: Refusing to answer lawful questions asked by a public servant is punishable.

title: Refusing to Sign Statement
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever refuses to sign a statement made by him when legally required to do so by a competent public servant commits an offence.
ai_summary: Refusing to sign a statement when legally required by a public servant is punishable.

title: False Statement on Oath or Affirmation
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever, being legally bound by an oath or affirmation to state the truth before a public servant or any person authorised by law to administer such oath or affirmation, makes a statement which is false and which he knows, believes to be false, or does not believe to be true, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Legal obligation to state the truth
Statement made under oath or affirmation
offence_category: False Evidence and Public Authority Offences
ai_summary: Making a false statement under oath or affirmation before an authorised person is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and fine.

title: False Information Intended to Cause Misuse of Public Servant's Lawful Power
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever knowingly gives false information to a public servant intending to cause, or knowing it is likely to cause, the public servant to misuse lawful powers to the injury, annoyance, or detriment of another person commits an offence.
covered_acts: Providing false information to cause action against an innocent person
Causing wrongful disciplinary action by a public servant
essential_ingredients: Intention to influence action of a public servant
Likelihood of injury or annoyance to another person
ai_summary: Knowingly giving false information to induce a public servant to misuse lawful authority is punishable.

title: Resistance to Lawful Taking of Property by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever resists the lawful seizure, attachment, recovery, or taking of property by a public servant, knowing or having reason to believe that the person is a public servant acting lawfully, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Action performed by a public servant
Knowledge that the person is a public servant
ai_summary: Resisting the lawful taking of property by a public servant is punishable with imprisonment up to six months, fine, or both.

title: Obstructing Sale of Property by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever intentionally obstructs any sale of property conducted under the lawful authority of a public servant commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Sale conducted by a public servant
ai_summary: Intentionally obstructing a lawful sale conducted by a public servant is punishable.

title: Illegal Bidding or Purchase at Public Sale
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever purchases or bids for property at a public sale on behalf of a person legally incapable of purchasing that property, or bids without intending to fulfil the obligations arising from the bid, commits an offence.
covered_acts: Bidding on behalf of a legally disqualified person
Purchasing on behalf of a legally incapable person
Bidding without intention to complete the purchase
essential_ingredients: Knowledge of legal incapacity of purchaser
ai_summary: Illegal bidding or purchasing at a public sale on behalf of a disqualified person is punishable.

title: Obstructing Public Servant in Discharge of Public Functions
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Obstruction connected with discharge of public functions
ai_summary: Voluntarily obstructing a public servant while performing official duties is punishable.

title: Omission to Assist Public Servant When Legally Bound
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever, being legally bound to assist a public servant in the execution of public duty, intentionally fails to provide such assistance commits an offence.

222(a).offence: General failure to provide legally required assistance.

222(b).offence: Failure to assist in execution of court process, prevention of offence, suppression of riot or affray, or apprehension of offenders or escapees.

ai_summary: Failure to provide legally required assistance to a public servant is punishable, with enhanced punishment in criminal justice and public order situations.

title: Disobedience to Order Duly Promulgated by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever knowingly disobeys an order lawfully promulgated by a public servant directing him to abstain from an act or regulate certain property commits an offence.

223(a).offence: Disobedience causing or likely to cause obstruction, annoyance, injury, or risk thereof.

223(b).offence: Disobedience causing or likely to cause danger to human life, health, safety, riot, or affray.

important_explanation: Intent to cause harm is not required. Knowledge of the order and likelihood of resulting harm is sufficient.
illustration_summary: Knowingly violating a lawful order restricting a religious procession and thereby causing danger of riot constitutes an offence.
ai_summary: Knowingly disobeying a lawful public order is punishable, with greater punishment when it endangers life or causes riots.

title: Threat of Injury to Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever threatens injury to a public servant or to a person in whom the public servant is interested for the purpose of influencing the exercise of public functions commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Target is a public servant or related interested person
Intent to influence official action, delay, or omission
ai_summary: Threatening a public servant to influence official functions is punishable with imprisonment up to two years, fine, or both.

title: Threat of Injury to Prevent Legal Application for Protection
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever threatens injury to a person in order to prevent that person from seeking legal protection from a public servant legally empowered to provide such protection commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Protection sought from a legally empowered public servant
ai_summary: Threatening a person to stop them from seeking legal protection is punishable with imprisonment up to one year, fine, or both.

title: Attempt to Commit Suicide to Compel or Restrain Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
content: Whoever attempts to commit suicide with the intention of compelling or restraining a public servant from discharging his official duty commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Intent to compel or restrain a public servant
Connection with discharge of official duty
ai_summary: Attempting suicide to pressure or prevent a public servant from performing official duties is punishable.

title: Giving False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, being legally bound by oath, law, or declaration to state the truth, makes a statement that is false and which he knows, believes to be false, or does not believe to be true, is said to give false evidence.
important_explanations: A statement may be verbal or otherwise.
False statements regarding one's belief are also false evidence.
A person may give false evidence by claiming belief in something he does not actually believe.
illustrations_summary: Knowingly false interpreter or translator certification.
ai_summary: Defines the offence of giving false evidence under oath, declaration, or legal obligation.

title: Fabricating False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever creates false circumstances, false entries, false records, false electronic records, or false documents intending that they appear as evidence and mislead a judicial or legal proceeding is said to fabricate false evidence.
covered_acts: Making false entries in books or records
Creating false circumstances intended for use as evidence
illustrations_summary: Planting stolen property to implicate another person.
Forging letters to falsely establish a criminal conspiracy.
ai_summary: Defines fabrication of false evidence intended to mislead courts or legal proceedings.

title: Punishment for False Evidence or Fabrication of False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Provides punishment for giving false evidence or fabricating false evidence.

229(1).offence: False evidence or fabricated evidence in a judicial proceeding.

229(2).offence: False evidence or fabricated evidence outside judicial proceedings.

important_explanations: Investigations preliminary to court proceedings are stages of judicial proceedings.
Court-directed investigations are also judicial proceedings.
ai_summary: False evidence in judicial proceedings attracts higher punishment than false evidence in non-judicial matters.

title: False Evidence Causing Conviction for Capital Offence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence intending to cause, or knowing it is likely to cause, the conviction of a person for a capital offence commits an aggravated offence.

230(1).offence: False evidence intended to cause conviction for a capital offence.

230(2).offence: An innocent person is convicted and executed because of such false evidence.

punishment.alternative_punishment: Life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years with fine under sub-section (1)

ai_summary: Giving false evidence that causes or is intended to cause conviction for a capital offence attracts extremely severe punishment, including death in the gravest cases.

title: False Evidence Causing Conviction for Serious Non-Capital Offence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence intending or knowing it is likely to cause the conviction of a person for an offence punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment of seven years or more, commits an offence.

punishment.description: Punished as if convicted of the offence for which the innocent person was intended to be convicted.

illustration_summary: Giving false evidence to secure conviction for dacoity may result in the same punishment applicable to dacoity.
ai_summary: A person who uses false evidence to secure conviction for a serious offence may receive the same punishment as that offence.

title: Threatening Person to Give False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever threatens another person with injury to person, reputation, or property, or to a person in whom that individual is interested, intending to compel the giving of false evidence, commits an offence.

232(1).offence: Threatening a person to induce false evidence.

232(2).offence: An innocent person is convicted and sentenced to death or imprisonment exceeding seven years because of such false evidence.

punishment.description: Offender receives the same punishment imposed on the innocent person.

ai_summary: Threatening someone to give false evidence is punishable, and may attract the same punishment imposed on an innocent victim wrongfully convicted.

title: Using False or Fabricated Evidence as Genuine
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use evidence which he knows to be false or fabricated as genuine evidence commits an offence.
ai_summary: Knowingly using false or fabricated evidence attracts the same punishment as giving or fabricating false evidence.

title: Issuing False Certificate
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever issues or signs a certificate required by law, knowing or believing that it contains a materially false statement, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Issuing a materially false certificate is punishable in the same manner as giving false evidence.

title: Using False Certificate as Genuine
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use a certificate which he knows to be materially false as if it were genuine commits an offence.
ai_summary: Using a false certificate as genuine attracts the same punishment as giving false evidence.

title: False Declaration Receivable as Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, in any declaration receivable as evidence by a Court, public servant, or other person authorised by law, makes a false statement regarding a material fact which he knows or believes to be false, or does not believe to be true, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Making a materially false declaration receivable as evidence is punishable in the same manner as giving false evidence.

title: Using False Declaration as True
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true any declaration which he knows to be materially false commits an offence.
important_explanation: A declaration remains covered even if inadmissible due only to some procedural informality.
ai_summary: Knowingly using a false declaration as genuine attracts the same punishment as giving false evidence.

title: Causing Disappearance of Evidence or Giving False Information to Screen Offender
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, causes evidence of that offence to disappear or gives false information with the intention of screening the offender from legal punishment commits an offence.

238(b).offence: Underlying offence punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years.

238(c).offence: Underlying offence punishable with imprisonment less than ten years.

imprisonment.maximum: One-fourth of maximum punishment prescribed for the underlying offence

illustration_summary: Helping a murderer hide a body to prevent punishment attracts up to seven years imprisonment and fine.
ai_summary: Destroying evidence or giving false information to protect an offender attracts punishment based on the seriousness of the underlying offence.

title: Intentional Omission to Give Information of Offence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, intentionally omits to provide information which he is legally bound to give commits an offence.
ai_summary: Intentionally withholding legally required information about an offence is punishable.

title: Giving False Information Regarding an Offence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, gives information relating to that offence which he knows or believes to be false commits an offence.
ai_summary: Knowingly giving false information about an offence is punishable with imprisonment up to two years, fine, or both.

title: Destruction or Concealment of Document or Electronic Record to Prevent Production as Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever secretes, destroys, obliterates, alters, or renders illegible any document or electronic record which he may lawfully be compelled to produce as evidence, with intent to prevent its production or use in a Court or lawful proceeding, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Document or electronic record capable of being required as evidence
Intent to prevent production or use as evidence
ai_summary: Destroying or concealing a document or electronic record to prevent its use as evidence is punishable.

title: False Personation in Judicial Proceedings
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever falsely impersonates another person and, in that assumed identity, makes statements, admissions, confesses judgment, becomes bail or security, or performs any act in a civil or criminal proceeding commits an offence.
ai_summary: Impersonating another person in a judicial proceeding is punishable.

title: Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property to Prevent Forfeiture or Execution
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever fraudulently removes, conceals, transfers, or delivers property or an interest in property to prevent its forfeiture, recovery of fine, or execution of a decree or order commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Intent to defeat forfeiture, fine recovery, or court execution
ai_summary: Fraudulently hiding or transferring property to defeat court orders or forfeiture is punishable.

title: Fraudulent Claim to Property to Prevent Forfeiture or Execution
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever fraudulently accepts, receives, claims, or deceptively asserts rights over property knowing he has no lawful claim, intending to prevent forfeiture, fine recovery, or execution of a decree, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Intent to obstruct forfeiture or execution proceedings
ai_summary: Fraudulently claiming property to obstruct legal recovery or forfeiture proceedings is punishable.

title: Fraudulent Decree or Order
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever fraudulently causes or allows a decree or order to be passed for an amount not due, for a larger amount than due, for property not lawfully claimable, or causes execution after satisfaction of the decree, commits an offence.
illustration_summary: Allowing a false decree in favour of another person to defeat the rights of a genuine creditor constitutes an offence.
ai_summary: Obtaining or permitting a fraudulent decree or execution order is punishable.

title: False Claim in Court
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever fraudulently, dishonestly, or with intent to injure or annoy any person makes in a Court any claim which he knows to be false commits an offence.
ai_summary: Knowingly making a false claim before a Court is punishable.

title: Fraudulently Obtaining Decree or Order
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever fraudulently obtains, executes, permits, or suffers a decree or order for money, property, or interest not lawfully due, or after satisfaction of such decree, commits an offence.
covered_acts: Permitting fraudulent decree in one's name
ai_summary: Fraudulently obtaining or executing a decree or order is punishable.

title: False Charge of Offence Made with Intent to Injure
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever institutes a criminal proceeding or falsely charges a person with an offence, knowing there is no lawful ground for such proceeding or accusation, commits an offence.

248(a).offence: False criminal proceeding or false accusation.

248(b).offence: False charge relating to an offence punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment of ten years or more.

ai_summary: Knowingly making a false criminal accusation is punishable, with enhanced punishment for serious offences.

title: Harbouring or Concealing Offender
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever harbours or conceals a person whom he knows or has reason to believe to be an offender, with intent to screen that person from legal punishment, commits an offence.

249(b).offence: Harbouring offender punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years.

249(c).offence: Harbouring offender punishable with imprisonment exceeding one year but not exceeding ten years.

imprisonment.maximum: One-fourth of the maximum punishment prescribed for the offence

exception: Does not apply where the harbouring or concealment is by the spouse of the offender.
ai_summary: Harbouring or concealing an offender to protect them from punishment is punishable, subject to a spouse exception.

title: Accepting Gratification for Concealing Offence or Screening Offender
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever accepts, agrees to accept, attempts to obtain, or receives gratification or restoration of property in consideration of concealing an offence, screening an offender, or refraining from prosecution commits an offence.

250(b).offence: Underlying offence punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years.

250(c).offence: Underlying offence punishable with imprisonment not exceeding ten years.

imprisonment.maximum: One-fourth of the maximum punishment prescribed for the offence

ai_summary: Accepting benefits in exchange for concealing an offence or protecting an offender is punishable according to the seriousness of the underlying offence.

title: Giving Gratification for Concealing Offence or Screening Offender
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever gives, offers, agrees to give, causes to be given, or restores property in consideration of concealing an offence, screening an offender from legal punishment, or refraining from prosecution commits an offence.

251(b).offence: Underlying offence punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years

251(c).offence: Underlying offence punishable with imprisonment not exceeding ten years

imprisonment.maximum: One-fourth of the maximum punishment prescribed for the offence

exception: Does not apply where the offence is legally compoundable.
ai_summary: Giving money, benefits, or property to conceal an offence or protect an offender is punishable according to the gravity of the underlying offence.

title: Taking Gratification for Helping Recover Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever accepts or agrees to accept gratification for helping recover movable property obtained through an offence, without using all reasonable means to secure the offender's arrest and conviction, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Accepting a reward for recovering stolen property without helping bring the offender to justice is punishable.

title: Harbouring Escaped Offender or Person Ordered to be Apprehended
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever harbours or conceals a person who has escaped lawful custody or is ordered to be apprehended, with intent to prevent apprehension, commits an offence.

253(b).offence: Underlying offence punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment for ten years

253(c).offence: Underlying offence punishable with imprisonment exceeding one year but not exceeding ten years

imprisonment.maximum: One-fourth of the maximum punishment prescribed for the offence

exception: Does not apply where the harbouring or concealment is by the spouse of the person.
ai_summary: Harbouring an escaped offender or wanted person is punishable according to the seriousness of the underlying offence.

title: Harbouring Robbers or Dacoits
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever knowingly harbours persons who are about to commit, or have recently committed, robbery or dacoity, with intent to facilitate the crime or protect them from punishment, commits an offence.
important_note: It is immaterial whether the robbery or dacoity is intended to be committed or has been committed inside or outside India.
exception: Does not apply where the harbouring is by the spouse of the offender.
ai_summary: Knowingly sheltering robbers or dacoits to assist them or protect them from punishment is punishable.

title: Public Servant Disobeying Law to Save Person from Punishment
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: A public servant who knowingly disobeys legal directions governing official conduct, intending to save a person from punishment, reduce punishment, or protect property from forfeiture or legal charge, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Accused must be a public servant
Intent to save offender or property from legal consequences
ai_summary: A public servant who intentionally violates legal duties to protect an offender or property from legal consequences is punishable.

title: Public Servant Framing Incorrect Record or Writing
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, being a public servant charged with preparing any record or writing, intentionally prepares it incorrectly to cause loss or injury, save a person from legal punishment, or save property from forfeiture or legal liability, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Accused must be a public servant
Intent to cause injury, save offender, or protect property
ai_summary: A public servant who knowingly prepares a false official record to cause injury or protect an offender commits an offence.

title: Public Servant Making Report, Order, Verdict or Decision Contrary to Law
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, being a public servant, corruptly or maliciously makes or pronounces any report, order, verdict, or decision in a judicial proceeding knowing it to be contrary to law, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Knowledge that decision is contrary to law
ai_summary: A public servant who knowingly gives an unlawful judicial decision commits an offence.

title: Corrupt or Malicious Commitment for Trial or Confinement
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, having legal authority to commit persons for trial or confinement, corruptly or maliciously commits or keeps a person in confinement knowing such action is contrary to law, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Authority to commit or confine persons
ai_summary: Wrongfully committing or confining a person while knowing it is unlawful is punishable.

title: Public Servant Intentionally Allowing Accused Person to Escape
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: A public servant legally bound to apprehend or confine a person charged with an offence commits an offence if he intentionally fails to apprehend, allows escape, or assists escape.

259(a).offence: Person charged with offence punishable with death

259(b).offence: Person charged with offence punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years

259(c).offence: Person charged with offence punishable with imprisonment less than ten years

ai_summary: A public servant who intentionally allows an accused person to escape is punishable according to the seriousness of the offence charged.

title: Public Servant Intentionally Allowing Convicted Prisoner to Escape
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: A public servant legally bound to apprehend or confine a convicted person or person lawfully committed to custody commits an offence if he intentionally fails to apprehend, allows escape, or assists escape.

260(b).offence: Prisoner serving life imprisonment or imprisonment of ten years or more

260(c).offence: Prisoner serving imprisonment less than ten years or lawfully committed to custody

ai_summary: A public servant who intentionally allows a convicted prisoner to escape faces punishment based on the prisoner's sentence.

title: Negligent Escape from Confinement by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound to keep any person charged with, convicted of, or lawfully committed for an offence in confinement, negligently allows such person to escape, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Accused must be a public servant
Duty to keep person in lawful confinement
ai_summary: A public servant who negligently allows a prisoner or accused person to escape from lawful confinement commits an offence.

title: Resistance to Lawful Apprehension or Escape from Lawful Custody
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever intentionally resists or obstructs his lawful arrest, or escapes or attempts to escape from lawful custody for an offence with which he is charged or convicted, commits an offence.
important_note: Punishment under this section is in addition to punishment for the original offence.
ai_summary: Resisting lawful arrest or escaping from lawful custody attracts separate punishment in addition to punishment for the original offence.

title: Resistance to Apprehension or Rescue of Another Person
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever intentionally resists the lawful apprehension of another person or rescues or attempts to rescue another person from lawful custody commits an offence.

263(b).offence: Person charged with offence punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years

263(c).offence: Person charged with offence punishable with death

263(d).offence: Person sentenced to life imprisonment or imprisonment of ten years or more

ai_summary: Helping another person avoid arrest or escape custody is punishable, with punishment increasing according to the seriousness of the person's offence.

title: Public Servant Omitting to Apprehend or Allowing Escape in Other Cases
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: A public servant legally bound to apprehend or confine a person, in cases not covered by Sections 259, 260, 261, or any other law, commits an offence by intentionally or negligently failing to apprehend or allowing escape.
ai_summary: A public servant who intentionally or negligently allows escape in cases not covered elsewhere commits an offence.

title: Resistance, Escape or Rescue in Other Cases
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever intentionally resists lawful apprehension, escapes or attempts escape from lawful custody, or rescues or attempts to rescue another person from lawful custody in cases not covered by Sections 262, 263, or any other law, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Resisting lawful apprehension or helping escape in miscellaneous cases is punishable.

title: Violation of Conditions of Remission of Punishment
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, having accepted a conditional remission of punishment, knowingly violates any condition upon which the remission was granted, commits an offence.

punishment.description: The offender shall undergo the original sentence if no part of it has been served, or the remaining unserved portion if part has already been served.

ai_summary: A person who violates conditions attached to remission of punishment must undergo the original or remaining sentence.

title: Intentional Insult or Interruption to Public Servant in Judicial Proceeding
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever intentionally insults or interrupts a public servant while the public servant is sitting in any stage of a judicial proceeding commits an offence.
ai_summary: Insulting or interrupting a public servant during a judicial proceeding is punishable.

title: Unlawfully Serving as Assessor
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, by personation or otherwise, intentionally causes himself to be returned, empanelled, sworn, or serves as an assessor knowing that he is not legally entitled to do so, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Knowingly serving as an assessor without legal entitlement is punishable.

title: Failure to Appear in Court After Release on Bail or Bond
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
content: Whoever, after being charged with an offence and released on bail bond or bond, fails without sufficient cause to appear before the Court according to the terms of the bond, commits an offence.
important_notes: Burden of proving sufficient cause lies on the accused.
Punishment is in addition to punishment for the original offence.
Court may also order forfeiture of bond.
ai_summary: Failure to appear in Court after release on bail or bond without sufficient cause is punishable.

title: Public Nuisance
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: A person commits public nuisance by doing any act or illegal omission that causes common injury, danger, annoyance, obstruction, or inconvenience to the public or persons exercising public rights.
essential_ingredients: Common injury, danger, annoyance, or obstruction
Affects public or persons exercising public rights
important_note: A public nuisance is not excused merely because it causes some convenience or advantage.
ai_summary: Section 270 defines public nuisance and its essential elements. Punishment is provided in subsequent sections.

title: Negligent Act Likely to Spread Infection of Disease Dangerous to Life
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: Whoever unlawfully or negligently does any act which he knows or has reason to believe is likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Knowledge or reason to believe infection may spread
ai_summary: Negligently or unlawfully spreading a life-threatening infectious disease is punishable.

title: Malignant Act Likely to Spread Infection of Disease Dangerous to Life
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: Whoever malignantly does any act which he knows or has reason to believe is likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Knowledge or reason to believe infection may spread
ai_summary: Deliberately performing an act likely to spread a dangerous infectious disease is punishable.

title: Disobedience of Quarantine Rule
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: Whoever knowingly disobeys any Government rule relating to quarantine of transport or regulation of communication between infected and non-infected places commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Transport or infectious disease control measures
ai_summary: Knowingly violating quarantine or disease-control regulations is punishable.

title: Adulteration of Food or Drink Intended for Sale
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: Whoever adulterates any article of food or drink so as to make it noxious, intending to sell it or knowing it is likely to be sold as food or drink, commits an offence.
ai_summary: Adulterating food or drink to make it harmful and intended for sale is punishable.

title: Sale of Noxious Food or Drink
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: Whoever sells, offers for sale, or exposes for sale any food or drink which is noxious or unfit for consumption, knowing or having reason to believe it is harmful, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Food or drink unfit for consumption
Knowledge or reason to believe it is harmful
ai_summary: Selling or offering harmful or unfit food or drink for sale is punishable.

title: Adulteration of Drugs or Medical Preparations
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: Whoever adulterates any drug or medical preparation so as to lessen its efficacy, change its operation, or make it noxious, intending or knowing that it may be sold or used for medicinal purposes as unadulterated, commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Efficacy reduced, operation changed, or made harmful
Intention or knowledge that it will be used as genuine medicine
ai_summary: Adulterating medicines or medical preparations is punishable.

title: Sale of Adulterated Drugs or Medical Preparations
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: Whoever knowingly sells, offers for sale, dispenses, or causes the use of an adulterated drug or medical preparation as genuine commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Sale, offer for sale, dispensing, or use
ai_summary: Knowingly selling or supplying adulterated medicines is punishable.

title: Sale of Drug or Medical Preparation as a Different Drug
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: Whoever knowingly sells, offers for sale, or issues a drug or medical preparation as a different drug or medical preparation commits an offence.
ai_summary: Selling or supplying one medicine as another medicine is punishable.

title: Corrupting or Fouling Water of Public Spring or Reservoir
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
content: Whoever voluntarily corrupts or fouls the water of any public spring or reservoir so as to make it less fit for its ordinary use commits an offence.
essential_ingredients: Water rendered less fit for ordinary use
ai_summary: Contaminating public water sources is punishable.

title: Making Atmosphere Noxious to Health
